90   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description Mode The Mode selection controls how the communications will execute.   The choices are either single mode with one analysis at a time, or multiple mode where analyses are stored in a temporary location until they are ready to be sent as a batch. The Single Mode of Remote Communications is used when each burn is to be transmitted, one at a time, either manually or automatically.   When the “Transmit each burn” item is checked in the System Parameters dialog, transmission is automatic (the Transmit controls are disabled). Upon completion of each burn, the burn data is transmitted to the remote computer. When the “Transmit each burn” item is not checked in the System Parameters dialog, transmission is under operator control (the Transmit controls are enabled).   When the operator selects the Transmit function, either by clicking the Transmit button, pressing the F2 key, or selecting Transmit from the Operations menu, the most recent burn is transmitted. The Multiple (Batch) mode of Remote Communications is used when groups of burns are to be transmitted.   Multiple (Batch) transmissions occur only when the operator selects the Transmit function, either by clicking the Transmit button, pressing the F2 key, or selecting Transmit from the Operations menu. All burns since the last transmission are transmitted at that time. If this is the first time the operator selected the transmit function, all burns since the Batch mode was enabled are transmitted.   A status message appears in the center pane at the bottom of the Spectroil software screen, indicating that burns are being transmitted, and changes when the transmission is complete. Protocol There are four different transmission protocols offered. While the actual connection and transmission protocol may be similar in some modes, the format of the data varies from protocol to protocol. V 4.2 This is a sophisticated communications protocol and data format option. A specialized receiver program must be running on the remote computer. Bi-directional handshaking and message acknowledgement occur on each transmission to insure data integrity.
June 30, 2014 |   M96301 Rev. B   91 Windows Pull Down Menu Options The data format includes field separator control characters, and message framing. It is the responsibility of the receiver program to parse the message and extract the desired data fields. Printer This is a single direction communications protocol and a simple data format option. No specialized receiver program is required, but the remote computer must signal that it is connected and ready by raising the DTR and RTS signals. Once these signals are high, transmission occurs whenever there is data to be sent. There is no message framing or acknowledgement. A printer connected by a suitably configured serial port is an acceptable remote computer for this protocol. The data format is identical to the standard single burn printout. Results This is a single direction communications protocol and a simple data format option. No specialized receiver program is required, but the remote computer must signal that it is connected and ready by raising the DTR and RTS signals. Once these signals are high, transmission occurs whenever there is data to be sent. There is no message framing or acknowledgement. A printer connected by a suitably configured serial port is an acceptable remote computer for this protocol. The data format is a series of field values, separated by spaces. Each burn is terminated by a carriage return. V 4.2 Format This is a single direction communications protocol and a simple data format option.   No specialized receiver program is required, but the remote computer must signal that it is connected and ready by raising the DTR and RTS signals. Once these signals are high, transmission occurs whenever there is data to be sent. There is no message framing or acknowledgement. A printer connected by a suitably configured serial port is an acceptable remote computer for this protocol. The data format is nearly identical to the V 4.2 data format, but without the message framing and field separating control characters. Required Handshake This sub-menu is only active when protocol V 4.2 has been selected.   In normal operation both handshakes, DSR (Data Set Ready) and CTS (Clear To Send) are selected. Transmit Max 9999.99 This box when selected provides the option to transmit analytical results up to 9999.99. When the box is not selected, the maximum results transmitted are 999.99. History File The history file can be enabled with a check mark. When enabled, the analyses sent to a remote computer are also stored on the hard disc of the Spectroil. This provides a convenient backup file if it is determined at some point that there was a problem with the
92   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description data transmission to the remote computer.   Alternatively, this file can also be used as a temporary storage place prior to sending the entire file to a remote computer manually such as with a floppy disc, e-mail, etc. The formats for the history file are identical to those described for the Protocol on the previous page. The History File name is the location of the data file on the Spectroil hard disc. Comm Port Setup The Comm Port Setup is a submenu of Remote Computer Setup.   It is activated by clicking on its button located beneath the Mode box.   When activated, it provides the options as shown below. Port The communications port used for the link to the remote computer is selected in this box. It is important to note that this port is on the SBC system. Because COM 1 is reserved for communications with the Spectroil software system, only ports COM 2 and above are offered. The connection to the remote computer should be made from the selected port on the SBC system. Speed The selected speed must be the same on both the SBC system and the remote computer. In most cases, 9600 bits per second is the maximum sustainable speed. Data Bits The number of data bits controls the transmission word size. The selected word size must be the same on both the SBC system and the remote computer. In almost all cases, 8 data bits is the proper selection.
June 30, 2014 |   M96301 Rev. B   93 Windows Pull Down Menu Options Parity The parity option controls the transmission word error checking. The selected parity must be the same on both the SBC system and the remote computer. In almost all cases, no parity is the proper selection. Stop Bits The number of stop bits option controls the transmission word framing. The selected number of stop bits must be the same on both the SBC system and the remote computer. In almost all cases, 1 (stop bit) is the proper selection. System/Sample ID This menu option produces a dialog that can be configured by the user to meet any combination of alpha-numeric characters for global sample identification. A sample can be identified by up to six field segments. This dialog permits the operator to choose the number of field segments used for the sample identification. Each field segment may be auto-incrementing, meaning that after the first sample number is entered and if all numbers that follow are in numeric order, they can be automatically filled in incrementing order saving time. The sequence of how the fields will appear can be determined. The size of each field can be configured and limited to 40 characters total. The last column of the sample I.D. dialog is for V4.2 protocol. This protocol is capable of storing up to two segments of the sample ID in the file. More than three segments are not permitted. That portion of the sample identification that has V4.2 assigned as segment 1 or segment 2 will be transmitted or stored under the V4.2 protocol. System/Standardization Samples This menu option produces a dialog that allows the entry of calibration standards used to control the offset and slope of the calibration curves.   During calibration, a series of oil standards are measured and these standards plotted against the intensities achieved by each element result in a calibration curve.   These calibration curves can be observed using the View pull down menu option.   During normal operation, slight differences in environment and consumables result in a shift from the original factory calibration curves. During the process of standardization, a series of standardization samples are measured and
94   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description compared to the factory calibration curve to determine if and what factor of correction should be applied.   This dialog permits the entry of the calibration standard names to be used for the daily standardization operation. System/Status This menu option provides a dialog that summarizes the status of the system.   All of the information contained in this status screen is automatically updated and is for information purposes only. Operator intervention is allowed to reset the User Spark Counter.   This counter is generally reset after every 2000 burns and is frequently used as a maintenance milestone.   When the instrument has reached 2000 burns, it is generally time to perform routine operator controlled maintenance such as thorough cleaning of the sample stand area, excitation source, ands maintenance of the tungsten electrodes.   After maintenance, the operator should reset the user counter and proceeds with normal daily maintenance functions. When File/New is selected, the software will lead the operator through preset steps under this pull down menu. Program/Program Parameters This pull down menu option results in a dialog which permits the entry of a unique program name and a description of this program. The program name cannot be more that 10 alpha-numeric characters. The program name is not case sensitive and may contain spaces. The program description can be up to 40 alpha-numeric characters, is not case sensitive and may include spaces. •   “Enable Additive Background Correction Enable” is a feature that controls which elements have no background correction and is primarily used in the commercial oil analysis application. •   “Enable Background Force Zero Enabled” is a feature used in military instruments and necessary to obtain a correlated result as part of the JOAP Program.
96   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description •   “Global Standardization Enabled” is a feature that will apply the global standards to the selected program (Program Name). •   “Oil Detection Enabled” is a system feature to determine if the operator has loaded a sample cap filled with oil in the sample stand or an empty sample cap. •   “RFS Program” is an optional feature that selects source parameters, readout mode and the analytical configuration for the analysis of a sample by Rotrode Filter Spectroscopy (RFS).   This function should only be used with the optional RFS accessory. •   The “Fuel” option provides the ability for the data readout to generate negative numbers.   This option is normally selected for fuel analysis programs and is used for the quality control of contaminated fuels. •   The “Print Data” and “Transmit Data” options are used to select which data is to be sent to a   local printer, remote computer or both.   The selections for Print data and Transmit Data are identical. •   If “All Burns” is selected, every analysis in very mode is sent to the selected output. •   If “All burns in PPM Readout Mode” is selected, only analyses in the PPM mode will be sent to the selected output. •   If “All Averages” is   selected, only average values for a series of analyses will be sent to the selected output.   This data output format is recommended for fuel analysis where two or more analyses are made to create an average. •   If “Only when Sample ID is present” is selected, only analyses preceded by a sample ID will be sent to the selected output. This data output format is used when only actual sample analyses are to be sent to the selected output and not other analyses such as standardization, check burns, warm-up burns, etc. Program/Channel Sequence This selection determines the elemental appearance sequence on the Analysis Program screen. Displayed on the right side of this dialog is a list of all channels that appear in the System/Hardware/Optical dialog.   These elements are listed in alphabetic order. June 30, 2014 |   M96301 Rev. B   97 Windows Pull Down Menu Options Note: Reference elements are either Hydrogen (H) or Carbon (C). Channel Configuration The Channel Configuration dialog box is broken up into six sections. The information for any channel is accessed by using the Element Symbol scroll box. Once an individual channel element has been selected, the channel information of each element can be viewed. •   The first characteristic in the dialog box is the Display Mode. Each channel’s elemental output must either be displayed in concentration or intensity while in PPM mode. All analytical channels are normally set to concentration mode and all references are set to intensity mode. •   The second characteristic is the Numeric Resolution. This determines how many decimals appear to the right of the decimal point when in the IEC PPM mode. The default is 2 decimal places.
98   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description •   The third characteristic is to identify the Channel Type. An element can be an analytical (Ana), reference (Ref), or a background (Bkg) channel. The next column, “Task,” allows you to select the measurement cycle the specified channel signal is measured. The “Reference” column is used to select a reference for an analytical channel. The channel parameters section controls five parameters associated with each channel. The first parameter determines if the channel will be active or inactive in this analytical program. The second parameter determines if this channel is displayed on the screen (only if the channel is active). An active channel can operate without being displayed on the screen, as in the case of interelement corrections. The third parameter determines whether an active channel is printed on the local printer. The fourth parameter determines whether an active channel is transmitted to a remote computer or data management system on board Spectroil. The fifth parameter determines if this channel will contribute to the calculation of profile position. These parameters may be unique to each program. The low expected value and high expected value are the low and high sample standard values found originally. These values do not change over time; therefore they are the Expected values to obtain. In practice, these values do, however, change as a result of the environment and consumables. Therefore during the standardization process, the current values are obtained and automatically inserted in the Obtained column for each element. From these values, the offset and factors are calculated for each element. Under normal conditions, the factor is expected to be in the 0.5 to 5.0 range. The last two sections of the dialog include Breakpoint and the Breakpoint Graph. These sections permit the entry and display of intensity ratio values obtained during factory calibration of a new instrument and its corresponding graph. The result is a calibration curve that may be viewed in the bottom left hand corner of the dialog box under the Breakpoint Graph tab. In order to maintain the integrity of the factory calibration installed in the Spectroil when it was originally manufactured, this dialog is password protected. If the instrument’s calibration is suspect, it is highly advised that the performance be discussed with the Service Department of Spectro Scientific. The calibration curve intensity ratio values are on the left side and the concentration Ratio values are on the right side. On the far left side is calculated the intensity and concentration ranges which represent 5% above and below the intensity ratio value and 10% above the concentration value. The Graph provides a basic graphic display of the calibration curve for each of the elements in the system. Pressing the Print icon will create a hard copy of this calibration curve if the printer driver is properly selected for the graphic (not generic) mode. If, technical discussions conclude that the calibration curves must be adjusted, a password will be calculated and instructions will be given to enter this password under the menu selection File/Password. Program Burn Parameters The burn parameters dialogs control several software aspects of the burn cycle.   The left hand side of each dialog has a navigation bar where the burn duration can be divided into different sequence tasks. The top of each screen has fields where Oil Detect parameters can be set.   The Oil Detect block has five fields (or segments). This block is part of the artificial intelligence and sample
June 30, 2014 |   M96301 Rev. B   99 Windows Pull Down Menu Options stand interlock monitoring hardware to protect the instrument from operator error.   The first segment is “Element” and the element designated in this field is always Hydrogen (H) and its output is used to determine the presence of hydrocarbon emission in the first few seconds of the burn cycle.   In the event the operator inadvertently installs a sample cap without oil or an insufficient amount of oil, the lack of Hydrogen output will trigger the termination of the burn cycle.   Selecting the “Enable Oil Detection” flag in the System Parameters dialog can activate this feature. Tasks are added on the navigation pane by double-clicking on a block in the navigation bar. The Spectroil is currently pre-configured to have four sequence tasks, a Pre-burn Setup Detect, Measure 1 and Measure 2. The first time sequence is the Preburn Time.   Preburn Time is 6 seconds (6000 ms), a cycle where the arc is initiated and the sample is introduced to the analytical gap for excitation. The purpose of the preburn period is to generate heat in order for the carbon disc electrode to absorb the sample until it becomes saturated. By the end of the preburn time, the sample temperature and analytical signal has reached stability and is suitable for measurement. The combined Measure1 and Measure 2 time is typically 24 seconds, 19 for measure 1 and 6 for Measure 2.   The interval is the time in milliseconds that the controller integrates the CCD.   With an interval of 100, Measure 1 thus integrates the CCD 1900 times in the 19 seconds of measure time.   During the Measure 1 and Measure 2 sequences the light intensity from the excitation process is measured and stored for each analytical channel. These intensities are used in a series of calculations to determine each elements concentration after background corrections have been made.
100   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description The other check boxes on the screens are currently not used and available for further development. Program/Reference Values This dialog permits the entry of typical intensity values for the reference channels.   These fixed values will be used as the dividend in the intensity ratio calculation. Military instruments have only one reference element and that is Hydrogen (H).   Commercial instruments may have a Hydrogen (H) and Carbon (C) reference channels.   Reference selection is performed at the factory before initial calibration, but may be changed in the field depending upon applications and performance. Program/Standardization Samples/Standardization Values This dialog presents a table where the values obtained from the initial factory calibration curves are entered as standardization points.   For every element, a low expected value is entered as well as a high expected value.   Technically these values are for the low and high
June 30, 2014 |   M96301 Rev. B   101 Windows Pull Down Menu Options sample standard that were designated in the previous sample name dialog.   These values are fixed and do not change over time; therefore they are the “Expected” values. In practice, these values do, however, change as a result of the environment and consumables. Therefore, during the standardization process, the current values are obtained and automatically inserted in the Obtained column for each element in order that a comparison can be performed between the Expected and Obtained values.   The comparison will result in a change in the standardization factors and offsets. Program/Standardization Samples/Standardization Factors This dialog displays the factor and offset values that have been automatically calculated as a result of completing a standardization procedure.   During the standardization procedure, intensity ratio values are obtained for the low and high standardization samples for all elements.   These values are listed under Standardization Values.   From these values, the offset and factors are calculated for each element.   Under normal conditions, the factor is expected to be in the 0.5 to 5.0 range. This is an information only screen and altering these values may have a significant effect on the instrument’s calibration. Program/Crossovers This dialog displays a table to enter the crossover point when one element wavelength exceeds its optimum analytical performance and a second wavelength of the same element
102   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description with superior performance in the higher concentration range is used. In these cases, the crossover elements are identified and listed. Program/Inter Element Corrections This dialog corrects the influence of one elements presence and concentration on another element.   Due to excitation characteristics and optical design, it is possible that one element may influence or interfere with another element. This interference has two conditions.   One element’s presence may enhance the presence of another element while another elements presence may suppress the presence of a second element.   This is known as additive or multiplicative interference.   This dialog permits the entry of the element with interference and the element causing the interference.   A choice of additive or multiplicative interference is available and the correction factor can be entered to null the interference.   These interferences are normally installed at the factory and do not require alteration. Program/Display Profile This dialog shows a log with the status of the last two optical profiles. The screen shows the status of each CCD chip in the optic.   This screen also appears automatically each time a
June 30, 2014 |   M96301 Rev. B   103 Windows Pull Down Menu Options profile is performed.   The profile function must be repeated if any of the chips status is not OK. These functions are standardization, profiling, etc. and do not result in one specific dialog but a series of dialogs to complete the operation. Operations/Multiple Display This menu option does not result in a dialog.   This menu selection is to permit the accumulation of up to twenty measurements to appear on the screen so an average can be calculated.   This is used for such functions as standardization, profiling and statistical analysis where the average of multiple measurements will be required, or when profiling, a comparison of nine measurements must be made.   This option is active when a check mark appears to the left of the words Multiple Display. Additive Background Correction indicates that some elements are not background corrected, therefore, a factor of 0.00000 is set in the factor column for that element and if present when the disc electrode offset procedure is performed, the calculated F/R Ratio value will not be set into the Factor column for that element. Databases The Spectroil software program supports transfer of burn data to a database program that resides on the spectrometer.   Currently four options exist.   Database transfer formats exist for AETC (a DOS based program for USAF applications), a generic database that generates a data transfer file, and a sample identification driven (SID) database. In all cases, the data transfer is accomplished by writing a file to the hard drive of the system after completion of a burn. In the AETC mode, the file contains a single entry, and will be overwritten by the next burn.   It is the responsibility of the user, or the database application, to retrieve the data from the hard drive before the next burn is made.   SID database creates a spreadsheet file for a user designated sample ID field.   The Generic transfer mode accumulates lines of data, one per burn, until it is deleted. It also provides for a substantial amount of user configuration. The appropriate database may be selected from the Databases menu.   When a database is active, a check mark will appear beside it. To de-activate a database, click on it a second time. Only one database may be active at a time. When one database is active, the menu items for the others are disabled. Databases/AETC When you select the AETC menu item, the dialog below appears. The dialog contains two fields. The first field contains the number of channels per line to appear on each text line in the transfer file (see below). The second field contains the full path and file name for the transfer of the data. This file will be overwritten after each burn. It is your responsibility, or the external database, to retrieve the data from the external file before the next burn is made. You have control of which elements are entered in the transfer file. Those elements checked under the Remote column of the Channel Parameter Dialog are listed in the transfer. The Channel Sequence Dialog specifies the order in which the elements appear. The transfer file is a text file, written in a fixed format. A sample of the file appears as follows:  ID = |Sample 01   |Mobil|   08/21/2000 09:38:00 Burns = 1   CS = Test V1.38   Mode = PPM K   Li   Zn   Na   Mg   MgHi   Si   H
110   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description  14.7 1.0 1134   35.6   778   778   383   4964 V   Cr   Mn   Ca   C   Ni   Cu   Al 617 4919 671   28.1 5153 5079 4843 474 VHi   Pb   Fe 4890   4385   1266  The file format is identical for each burn, facilitating software parsing. The fields “ID =”, “BURNS =”, “CS =” and “Mode =” appear in the same position in each file. They provide pointers to the Sample ID, burn number, curve set (program) and readout mode respectively. The date and time will always appear following the sample ID. Following the heading area will be the data from the specific burn. The data will appear in lines, with the number of elements in each line corresponding to the value entered in the “Number of channels per line” field in the AETC Setup Dialog. The elements selected for display will be grouped in pairs of lines. The first line of each pair will contain the element symbol. The second line will contain the readout value for that element. A blank line will separate groups of lines. Databases/Generic When you select the Generic menu item, the dialog shown below appears.   The dialog contains a sequence of fields that provide control of the data to appear in the transfer file, as well as the name and path of the transfer file. The first field contains the full path and file name to be used for the transfer of the data. This file will accumulate a single text line after each burn. The file will continue to grow,
June 30, 2014 |   M96301 Rev. B   111 Windows Pull Down Menu Options containing a single line for each burn, until the user, or the external database deletes it. If the file does not exist, Spectroil software will create it when it is needed. The second field is “Elements to include” and has three choices: On Screen, Remote or All. Three choices are offered by the dialog: “On Screen,” “Remote,” or “All.” Only one button may be checked. In all cases, the Channel Sequence Dialog specifies the order in which the elements appear. When you select the On Screen button, the same elements that appear on the Spectroil software screen appear in the transfer file. When you select the Remote button, those elements checked under the Remote column of the Channel Parameter Dialog are listed in the transfer. When you check the All button, all elements appear in the transfer file. The third field is the “field separator character” which allows you to specify the character used to separate fields in the transfer file. This character may be any single ASCII character. In the example, it is a comma (,).   It is your responsibility to select a character, which will not appear in the data stream. Following the separation character are the enabling options for the Date, Sample ID, and the Element names.   Each field checked will appear in the transfer file. The transfer file is a text file, written in a fixed format. A sample of the file, as configured by the dialog box settings in the illustration, appears as follows:  Al,38.99,Cr,26.74,Cu,358.20,Mg,10.45,Na,15.45,Ni,4Al,38.99,Cr,26.74,Cu,358.20,Mg,10.45,Na,15.45,Ni,47.76,Pb,3 9.97,Si,25.57,Sn,56.48,Ti,35.56,B,28.72,Mo,65.50,Zn,293.05,Ba,62.98,P,141.96,Cd,17.67,K,48.70,H,4851,Bkg,6510 ,Ag,8.38,DATE,10/25/2004,ID,This is the Sample ID   ,So Is This   ,  The file is a series of text lines, one line per burn, with a carriage-return line-feed pair (ASCII 0D 0A) at the end. The line breaks in the illustration do not occur in the transfer file. The first data on a line will always be the readout data from the specific burn. If the Element names have been enabled, the elements selected for display will be grouped in pairs of fields.   The first field of each pair will contain the element symbol. The second field will contain the readout value for that element.   The chosen field separator character will appear after each field.   There will be a trailing separator character after the last field. If the Elements names have not been enabled, the elements selected for display will appear as a series of single fields.   The chosen field separator character will appear after each field. Following the data fields will be the date, if it is enabled.   The character string “DATE” will appear, followed by a field separator character.   Then the date will appear, in MM/DD/YYYY format, followed by a field separator character.   When the date is not enabled, neither the “DATE” nor the date field will appear. Following the actual date is the Sample ID.   The character string “ID” will appear, followed by a field separator character.   Then the sample ID will appear, followed by a field separator character. When the sample ID is not enabled, neither the “ID” literal nor the sample ID field will appear. If the Date, Sample ID, and Element names options have not been enabled, the same   burn would appear like this:  38.99,26.74,358.20,10.45,15.45,47.76,39.97,25.57, 56.48,35.56,28.72,65.50,293.05,62.98,141.96,17.67, 48.70,4851,6510,8.38,DATE,10/25/2004,ID, This is the Sample ID   ,So Is This   ,
112   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description Sample Identification Driven (SID) Database The Sample Identification Driven (SID) database allows you to create data base files in spreadsheet format based on any part of a user selected Sample ID field.   These user selectable delineated files with analysis data can be stored on the Spectroil’s internal hard disk for further evaluation, or stored temporarily for transfer to an external computer or network.   When stored on the Spectroil’s hard disk, all future analyses for the selected Sample ID field will be stored and appended to the past analyses in that file (spreadsheet). SID Setup To use the SID database, the Spectroil’s system Sample ID must be setup to include the field that is to be used to establish the spreadsheet file. From the System pull down menu on the Analysis Program screen, select “Sample I.D……”. In the Sample I.D. Setup screen, verify that the sample ID is configured per your requirements. Because the SID database will allow the choice of any of the name fields to be used for dynamic file naming, make sure to have a name for all working sample ID segments.
June 30, 2014 |   M96301 Rev. B   113 Windows Pull Down Menu Options SID Configuration The SID database is initiated from the Database pull down menu on the Analysis Program screen.   To configure the SID database, select “SID” from the Databases pull down menu. The first time that the database is setup, the SID Database Transfer File Setup screen appears with empty fields. An example dialog box with explanation for the fields is shown below.
114   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description • Enter the full path for the transfer file: This is the location on the Spectroil’s hard disk where the database file is going to be stored.   In our example, the file path is C:\Data Files\. • Sample ID for file name: This drop down menu lists the names of all available sample ID segments that can be used for the spreadsheet file name. The screen below is a sample of the file names available in our example. • File extension: This is the extension to be used for the file name, our sample will use “csv” for spreadsheets. • Elements to include: The analytical elements that will the included in the database file are determined by the setup in the menu Program>Channels>Parameters.   In our example, the elements checked off for “Remote” transfer will be included in the SID database. • Field separator character: The character entered will be placed between each field of data.   In our example, a “,” (comma) is used. • Include Date:   The date of the analysis is included in the database along with the analytical data. • Include Time: The time of the analysis is included in the database along with the analytical data. The SID database will be enabled once “OK” in the dialog box is selected. SID Database Routine Use The SID database can be used to store analytical data from routine samples after it has been configured. Analyses will be stored in files based on the Sample ID selected in the data base setup. 1.   On the Analytical program screen, verify that the SID database is active.   A check mark must be to the left of “SID” in the Databases pull down menu. 2.   Enter the sample ID by clicking on the ID icon on the Analysis Program screen, or pressing function key F3.   In the Sample ID Entry screen, enter the fields corresponding to the file where the data is to be stored, and any other user selected fields.
June 30, 2014 |   M96301 Rev. B   115 Windows Pull Down Menu Options In our example, data will be stored by Unit S/N (Unit Serial Number). Select “Single” if you are analyzing samples manually (“Multiple” is for applications where a robot is used to analyze samples). The sample ID will appear in the upper left of the Analysis Program Screen. Note: A file is not created if the sample ID field is blank.   Also, if the field contains non-file nameable characters (/, @, *, etc.), the file will be saved with an _ (underscore) replacing the character. 3.   Press the Start Burn icon on the Analysis Program screen or function key F9 to start the analysis of the sample. 4.   Once the analysis is complete, a file is created and stored on the Spectroil’s hard disk in the location specified in the SID database Transfer File Setup.   In our example the files are stored on drive C in a folder called “Data Files.” You can open the files and view the data in a standard spreadsheet format, as shown in the example below:
116   June 30, 2014   |   M96301 Rev. B 6   Spectroil Software Description Tools The Tools menu has functions for use by Spectro certified maintenance personnel.   The functions are not available to the user. Help The Help menu contains Help Topics and About Spectroil. This section will describe each operation in general. Help/Help Topics This menu option opens an Adobe ®   Acrobat file that contains a Quick Help Section and the entire Spectroil Operation and User Maintenance Manual. Links to summarized operating procedures are provided from the main Quick Help page along with links to the corresponding detailed section in the manual. The table of contents from the Operation and User Maintenance Manual can also be used to quickly jump to the corresponding section in the manual. Each table of content section is linked to the appropriate page in the manual and access is provided by clicking on the link. The entire Operation and User Maintenance Manual, or sections thereof, can also be printed if a hard copy of the document is preferred. Help/About Spectroil This dialog provides the date and version of the software release. It also displays additional Spectro Scientific information. Check the Spectro Scientific web site (http://www.spectrosci.com) or the listing of the most current version number. Software upgrades can be obtained at no additional cost.  Caution:   Do not open a file where data is stored while analyses are in process. No data will be stored in the file.

SpectroLNF Q200   v   July 2012/Version 5.1  Product Safety  WARNING: To avoid the risk of fire or explosion, do not use volatile solvents such as hexane, heptane, naphtha, mineral spirits, toluene, or gasoline (petrol) in the Automatic Sample Processor (ASP). Volatile solvents also emit noxious vapors that the operator should not breathe. CAUTION: The Database Edit feature is intended only to correct mistaken entries. Do not use it to add sample information that was not available at the time the sample was analyzed, as changing sample record information will affect the ultimate diagnosis.

July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   1  1  Introduction  1.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Overview  The SpectroLNF Q200 is a bench-top instrument that analyzes hydraulic and lubricating oil samples from various types of equipment and machinery as part of a condition monitoring program. The monitoring is based primarily on the morphological analysis of the abnormal wear particles that are created from the inter- nal components of the machine. As a secondary application, the SpectroLNF Q200 is also an excellent parti- cle counter. The operator is presented with an assessment of particles found in the fluid sample and a history of previous results for the same equipment. The Q200 now has the ability to measure dynamic viscosity (in cP) of used oil samples. The SpectroLNF Q200 consists of two main components: •   A bench-top instrument that processes the sample (A) connected to a Personal Computer via a USB cable. •   A computer (B) to operate the instrument and to manage the analytical data.  NOTE: REGISTER YOUR LNF Q200 BY CONTACTING SUPPORT@SPECTROINC.COM TO RECEIVE THE LATEST SOFTWARE UPDATES.  A B  Figure: 1-1: SpectroLNF Q200  A B

NOTE:  Spectro Incorporated typically delivers the SpectroLNF Q200 with a Personal Computer. The computer supplied may, however, vary depending on availability. The customer has the option to supply their own computer. •   Pentium II ®   500 MHz processor •   128MB RAM •   10GB hard disc •   Windows   ®   2000 or XP operating system •   USB port   •   An RS232 port (or second USB port with an RS-232 adapter)  NOTE:  Computer comes separated from the main unit. July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   3 Introduction  1.5   Compliance/Approval 1.6   Book Conventions  The following definitions apply to specific instructions throughout this manual.  1.6.1   Warning 1.6.2   Caution 1.6.3   Note  ISO 4406 (1999), NAS 1638 NAVAIR 01-1A-17, AS 4509, NAS 1638 (NEW) The SpectroLNF Q200 is calibrated to length in the image plane and does not require calibration using NIST SRM 2806 because it directly images the particles in real time. The calibration procedure ISO 11171 is designed for conventional particle counters and does not apply to the SpectroLNF Q200. The SpectroLNF Q200 correctly measures the par- ticles in the NIST SRM 2806 standard which is the same fluid used to calibrate a conventional optical particle counter. An original verifica- tion curve made with a certified ISO standard (NIST SRM 2806) is included. CE Approval   The SpectroLNF Q200 is CE approved. 4   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Introduction
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   5  2  System Description  2.1   Operational Overview  SpectroLNF Q200 oil samples are processed individually, one after the other. In the default processing time, a sample run takes approximately 2 minutes: •   Sample shaking and degassing (4.3 minutes - this can be done while samples are being processed) •   Draw the sample into the flow cell (30 sec) •   Actual analysis (30 sec) and •   Rinse the cell and flow path (~ 1 minute).  2.1.1   Analysis Steps  1.   A user must log into the system in order to start the analysis process. 2.   The user progresses through a series of screens, entering appropriate data for the sample and for the machine/unit from which the sample was taken. 3.   Once the sample information is entered, the user should vigorously shake the sample bot- tle for at least thirty seconds and then place it in an ultrasonic bath to coalesce and liberate as many air bubbles as possible. 4.   Place the bottle under the sipper tube and direct the SpectroLNF Q200 to continue This begins the process of drawing the sample into the instrument.  NOTE:  Machine specific information (such as model or serial number) need only be entered once and subsequently recalled by reference to the machine’s number. Other information that is frequently used (such as fluid type) also need to be entered only once and then subsequently selected from a list of previous entries.  NOTE:  The operator also has the option to enter just a lab number to run a sample. This option was included in the operating software for those who wish to export the SpectroLNF Q200 data to another database that already contains machine/unit information. This feature avoids dupli- cation of machine/unit information.  NOTE:  Highly viscous samples may require longer shaking and more time in the ultrasonic bath to remove air bubbles. An ultrasonic bath with a power density of at least 4000 W/m 2   is recommended.
6   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 System Description  5.   After the sample has been processed, the operator may choose to: •   View the results in terms of maximum diameter or circular diameter. •   Display video frames of selected wear particles that were captured during the run, or •   Display the trending information and machine diagnosis that results from the current sample. 6.   After the operator reviews the results, the operator can choose to accept and validate the results. 7.   If the results are accepted, the sample information is stored: •   In a database on the local computer or •   the information can be exported to another computer, such as one running a LIMS (Lab- oratory Information Management System) software program.
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   7 System Description  2.2   SpectroLNF Q200 Interior Components 2.2.1   Sipper Assembly Figure: 2-1: Sipper Assembly Components  Name   Description  Sipper Assembly   The sipper assembly can be raised to accommodate the fluid sample bottle. It contains the tube that is immersed in the fluid in order to draw the sample into SpectroLNF Q200 for analy- sis. A replaceable mesh filter to trap fibers and extremely large particles is located in the top of the assembly. A   Sipper Tube   Fluid sample is drawn through this tube into the SpectroLNF Q200 unit. B   Sipper Head   A sliding head that supports the sipper and inlet tubes and filter in the sipper assembly. C   Filter Release Screw   Unscrewing this connection exposes the inline filter. D   Sipper Output Tube   Fluid from the Sipper tube is routed through this tube toward the flow cell.  A B C  Figure: 2-2: Sipper Assembly View  D
8   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 System Description  Figure: 2-3: Components - Front and Right Side  Name   Description  A   Collimator Lens   A lens that modifies the incoming laser beam to optimize it for SpectroLNF Q200 measurements. B   Laser Fiber Optic   This fiber conducts the laser light from the laser to the flow cell. C   Flow Cell   The flow cell is the heart of the SpectroLNF Q200 flow path. Sample flows from the sample bottle up the sipper tube, through a 100 μm filter screen, through a bulk head connector, through tubing to the flow cell, through tubing to another bulk head fitting, through the peristaltic pump and finally through more tubing to the drain bottle. There is a bypass D   Flow Cell Holder   The flow cell holder keeps the flow cell in position and can be opened in order to replace or install a new cell. E   4X Magnifying lens (Hidden) The 4X-macro lens images the sample fluid in the flow cell onto the CCD camera. F   Laser Kill Switch (Safety Interlock) Magnetic safety switch that activates to remove power to the laser when the side panel is removed. G   Power Supply   The power supply provides the required voltages for the opera- tion of SpectroLNF Q200. Main instrument input power can be either 110/220 VAC, 50 or 60 Hz. H   Bypass Valve   A software activated valve that bypasses fluid past the flow cell. When enabled, it is activated automatically during start sample or flush sample. Because of the restrictive flow cell size, the bypass speeds up sample flow to reduce total analysis time. I   Peristaltic Pump   The peristaltic pump is located on the front of the SpectroLNF Q200 chassis. It provides the suction to pull the sample through the flow path. The processing software controls its operation. J   Cell Output Tube   Fluid exiting the flow cell goes through this tube. K   Discharge Tube   The discharge tubing connects to a waste bottle/container to receive the sample fluid after it has passed though the Spec- troLNF Q200. It is recommended that a suitably large waste container be used to collect the discharge from more than just a few samples. A location below the SpectroLNF Q200 and on the floor is preferred.

July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   13 System Description  2.3   SpectroLNF Q200 Quick Start Setup Instructions  The SpectroLNF Q200 operating software and device drivers have been pre-installed onto the PC supplied with your system. To begin using the SpectroLNF Q200, please follow these instructions: 1.   Connect the USB cable included with the system into any of the USB ports located on the back of the PC. The other end of the cable is connected to the back of the SpectroLNF Q200 2.   Connect the power cord to the input line voltage connector on the back of SpectroLNF Q200. 3.   Plug the keyboard and mouse into the remaining USB connections on the back of the PC. 4.   Connect the power cable to a convenient wall outlet. 5.   Turn ON the PC. It will automatically boot into Windows ®   . 6.   If applicable, at the Administrator Password screen type in the following username and password (without the quotation marks): Username “Administrator” Password “pass” 7.   When Windows   ®   has finished booting, turn ON the SpectroLNF Q200 with the ON/OFF switch on the back. The light on the SpectroLNF Q200 front panel will turn green. A green sloped arrow will appear in the bot- tom right control panel on the monitor indicating that the USB port on the PC has detected a connection to the SpectroLNF Q200. 8.   Double-click the icon on the desktop called “LasernetUSB” to start the SpectroLNF Q200 software. The software will carry out a series of self-checks on the SpectroLNF Q200 before loading the main Spec- troLNF Q200 Start Screen.  NOTE:  If you did not receive a computer from Spectro that is configured to run the SpectroLNF Q200 see “SpectroLNF Q200 Software Installation Instructions” on page 8-183.  NOTE:  The SpectroLNF Q200 power supply can automatically adjust the input voltage and accommodate either 110 VAC or 220 VAC automat- ically. Most PC’s have the same feature, however some may require a switch setting near the power connection to set it to the local voltage.
14   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 System Description  9.   The SpectroLNF Q200 is now ready for use, as a powerful particle shape classifier and particle counter.  2.4   Circular Diameter, Maximum Diameter and Particle Counting Background  The SpectroLNF Q200 uses a direct imaging technique and is hence able to measure the size of particles in terms of either a maximum diameter or by a more traditional method called equivalent circular diameter. The equivalent circular diameter is equal to the diameter of circle that has the same area as the measured par- ticle. This is the measurement that the majority of all automatic particle counters have been using for the past decade to evaluate the cleanliness of hydraulic fluids. This incidentally was why the term “Hydraulic” is used to label the tab in the main results screens, see “Analytical Results Screens” on page 4-38.  NOTE:  At this time, or soon thereafter, analyze a check fluid (see “Test System / Set Screen” on page 4-49). This will verify that the system is opera- tional and did not sustain any damage during shipment. Also please send a copy of the report to our service department at support@spec- troinc.com.  Figure: 2-8: Start Screen
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   15 System Description  Cleanliness codes such as ISO, NAS and NAVAIR have been formulated over the years based on the equiva- lent circular diameter counts in certain size ranges. These are displayed on the SpectroLNF Q200 output screens along with size histograms showing the present versus previous result for each size range measured. For further explanation of these codes, see “Analytical Results Screens” on page 4-38. The SpectroLNF Q200 also uses a measurement called maximum diameter (or, maximum chord length) to measure the size of the particles it processes. The results can be viewed by clicking the “Wear Summary” tab, see “Main Results Screen” on page 4-38. The maximum diameter is determined by the maximum chord length (the longest straight line) between any two points on the particle.  2.4.1   Question?  If a trained metallurgist were to try and assess the size of a group of similar wear particles using an optical microscope, would he use equivalent circular diameter or simply measure the length of the maximum chord between two points of a particle? The answer is that he would measure the maximum length of each particle. Using an equivalent circular diameter measurement to ascertain the size of severe wear particles to diagnose a wear mode will give a much-underestimated result in terms of the size and the severity of the wear mode under investigation.  NOTE:  It is still possible to view the results based on circular diameter in the hydraulic screen even if you have run a lubricating oil through the sys- tem. It merely presents the results based upon circular diameter for par- ticles that were in the oil.  Figure: 2-9: LaserNet Fines Login Screen

July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   17  3  Processing Samples  3.1   Software Conventions  The SpectroLNF Q200 software is very logical and easy to use. Each menu has a dialog box that explains the action required of the operator. Warnings are posted in yellow dialog boxes. Entry buttons are gray, and active buttons display text in bold letters, while inactive buttons have text that appears washed out. In general: •   Buttons that cancel a screen or return to the previous screen are in the lower left •   Buttons that continue the normal processing steps are in the lower right of the screen. •   If a particular screen requires a sequence of entries, then the button requiring an input will be high- lighted in green and the other buttons will be disabled. The operator must enter the required information in the green highlighted button before proceeding. Each operational screen presents the user with a message area where text instructions describe the next input required from the operator. Most operator inputs are limited to selecting a button option or selecting from a list of choices. The SpectroLNF Q200 assembles the choices from previous operator responses to each query. For example, when selecting a Machine, the operator is prompted to select from a list of previous entries, or to create a new Machine entry. Once the new Machine is entered, it becomes part of a list of Machines from which the operator can select when processing the next oil sample.  3.1.1   Preparing Samples for Analysis  As in most analytical tests, the analysis is only as good as the sample. If we assume that a lubricant sample was properly taken from a machine, the next step prior to analysis with the SpectroLNF Q200 is to properly prepare the sample. Proper sample preparation includes shaking the sample container to get the wear particles into suspension in the oil and then putting the sample bottle into an ultrasonic bath to get rid of air bubbles. It should be noted that more viscous samples such as heavy-duty gear oils should be shaken for a longer period of time. Also, samples that have been in storage or were in transit for extended periods of time should be shaken for longer periods than samples just taken from a machine. The use of an automated shaker can help this process. For more information on types of shakers available please contact a sales representative at Spectro Incorporated. The SpectroLNF Q200 can handle a wide range of fluid viscosities. However, heavy fluids (viscosities greater than 150 cSt at 40°F) may require dilution with a solvent to make them flow better through the Spec- troLNF Q200 and to improve statistical results. Refer to Appendix B, on page B-1 for information on dilution requirements for various fluid types. The dilution factor is entered into the software during the sample set-up process and analytical correction for the dilution factor is automatic. The sample should be shaken for at least 30 seconds depending on viscosity. Immediately thereafter, the operator must place the sample bottle into an ultrasonic bath to remove air bubbles. The particle recognition software of the SpectroLNF Q200 automatically removes air bubbles greater than 20 μm from the counts using its pattern recognition algorithms. However, air bubbles less than 20   μ m will be counted as particles thus causing erroneously high counts. Therefore, as many bubbles as possible need to be removed using an ultrasonic bath.
18   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Processing Samples  3.2   Analyzing Samples 3.2.1   Overview  The step-by-step analysis process is detailed in section “3.3 LaserNet Fines: Steps” on page 3-18. Please also refer to for solvents used for flushing the sample cell, and see “Analyzing Phosphate Ester based fluids (Skydrol LD-4, 500B-4, Skydrol 5)” on page 3-23 when synthetic fluids such as phosphate esters, polygly- cols and skydrol are analyzed. The total sample processing time is dependent on the data acquisition parameters chosen. The total time is based on the worst-case sample process time of 2.1 minutes. If a viscosity measurement is performed the total time can increase by an additional 10 seconds to 2 minutes (sample and chosen parameters dependent). During this processing time, the instrument captures video images, or frames, as the sample flows through the flow cell. It is important to note that the shaking and ultrasonic degassing time is eliminated after the first sample by running these tasks while the current sample is processing/flushing. These times are worst case for highly sooted and viscous fluids (150 cst). The initialization and flush time will also vary depending on fluid viscosity and contamination.  3.2.2   Step-by-Step Sample Processing Procedure  The steps involved in the analysis of a sample are described in the table that follows. For maximum viscosity accuracy use PF   ®   Solvent (see “Viscosity Measurements:” on page 3-23). Other low volatility fluids such as clean diesel fuel or clean jet aviation fuel work equally well. July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   23 Processing Samples  When using the unique Skydrol Phosphate Ester tubing configuration,   flush with isopropyl alcohol (IPA) only.  For problem samples and in cases where the above recommended solvents are ineffective, acetone or ethyl alcohol or isopropyl alcohol can be used to clean the inside of the cell. In all instances, it is important that the solvents are completely flushed from the cell and not left in the cell for long periods of time.  3.3.2   Viscosity Measurements:  Spectro Inc recommends High Flash Point PF solvent for this application. High Flash Point PF solvent. If you have difficulty obtaining this solvent please contact Spectro support (support@spectroinc.com). Beyond being an excellent solvent for accurate Q200 viscosity measurements, PF   ®   Solvent also offers many safety and environmental advantages: PF   ®   Solvent is a full strength, penetrating degreaser having the following properties: •   Flash point above 140°F (60°C) •   Non-conductive and no residue •   Does not contain chlorinated solvents such as trichloroethylene or perchloroethylene •   Can be used in parts washers, dip tanks, and ultrasonic cleaners •   Safe to use on most surfaces •   No hazardous air pollutants  3.3.3   Analyzing Phosphate Ester based fluids (Skydrol LD-4, 500B-4, Skydrol 5)  Care needs to be taken when analyzing Phosphate Ester based fluids like Skydrol on the SpectroLNF Q200. These types of fluids are classified as “fire resistant” hydraulic fluids and are mainly used in the aviation industry. The material compatibility of these fluids is very different compared to the majority of hydrocarbon based oils and solvents routinely analyzed on the Q200. Spectro offers the following options for analyzing Phosphate Esters on the Q200. The various options are based on the amount of Phosphate Ester samples rou- tinely analyzed on a daily basis on the system in conjunction with mineral based oils.  3.3.3.1   Option 1- Infrequent Use of Phosphate Esters with Mineral Based Oils  The following guidelines should be strictly followed when analyzing Phosphate Ester based hydraulic oils on the SpectroLNF Q200 in its standard, shipped tubing configuration. These guidelines should be followed if a WARNING: Although more volatile solvents, such as hexane, heptane, naphtha, mineral spirits, toluene or gasoline (petrol) will serve the purpose of cleaning the flow cell and flow path, the use of these volatile solvents is strongly discouraged because of their flammability. Volatile solvents will also emit noxious vapors that the operator should not breathe. WARNING: Under no circumstance should volatile solvents such as hexane, hep- tane, naphtha, mineral spirits, toluene or gasoline (petrol) be used in the Automatic Sample Processor (ASP) as there is a risk of fire or explosion.
24   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Processing Samples  Phosphate ester fluid is analyzed very occasionally together with mineral based oils. It requires no initial hardware or tubing change. 1.   Disable Bypass - Ensure the bypass valve is disabled. The flow cell bypass is disabled by deselect- ing it on the sample processing tab in the configuration settings. Neglecting to disable the valve will cause it to seal shut.   Remember to enable it when running normal mineral based oils with standard solvents.  2.   Flush - In the standard shipped tubing configuration always flush the oil from the system using the recommended solvents (Q200 users manual section 3.3.1, page3-6). NEVER allow a Phosphate Ester fluid to sit in the flow path for prolonged periods of time. 3.   Replace Tubing -The standard Tygon (yellow) tubing and Viton (black) tubing is classi- fied as having poor material compatibility with Phosphate Esters – If Phosphate Ester flu- ids are analyzed ensure that all the tubing in the system is completely replaced after every 25 samples. This includes the peristaltic pump tubing.  3.3.3.2   Option 2 -  Multi purpose internal tubing configuration for use with Phosphate Esters and Mineral based oils. Requires additional solvents and interchanging of pump tubing together with optional bypass valve upgrade. This tubing configuration and “optional” bypass valve hardware change is recommended when mineral oils and Phosphate Ester fluids are analyzed equally in heavy volumes on the Q200. The internal tubing is replaced with a flexible polyethylene (Flexelene FX) tube which is compatible with both oil types and sol- vents and does not require time consuming frequent replacement. However, in this mode careful attention needs to be paid to the pump tubing and flushing solvents when switching between the different oils. The tubing will need to be interchanged between Viton and EPDM tub- ing depending on the type of oil being analyzed. Failing to not use the correct pump tubing for the relevant oil and/or the incorrect solvent will result in swelling and bursting of the pump tubing. CAUTION: An operating procedure or practice that may cause damage to the Spec- troLNF Q200 if not carefully observed or followed  Figure: 3-11: Sample Processing Tab - Configuration Settings
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   25 Processing Samples  The following should be strictly adhered to:  ALWAYS   flush the system with solvent after analyzing a Phosphate Ester and never let it sit for prolonged periods of time in the system In this configuration the bypass valve can either be replaced with an optional Kalrez modified valve ( RECOMMENDED ) or disabled in the software as described above.  3.3.3.3   Option 3  Unique Phosphate Ester (Skydrol) Solution – Tubing NOT compatible with mineral based oils and solvents. This tubing configuration allows for only Phosphate Ester based fluids to be analyzed on the Q200. This tub- ing uses specially formulated ethylene propylene, synthetic rubber (EPDM) that has excellent material com- patibility with these fluid types. EPDM tube does not cause swelling and it will allow Phosphate Esters to be analyzed for long periods without requiring frequent tubing changes –. Note: It is not recommended that min- eral based or synthetic samples are analyzed routinely in this tubing configuration. Also, in this tubing configuration Isopropyl Alcohol (IPA) should be the   ONLY   flushing solvent (section 3.3.1, page 3-6) used.   DO NOT   flush with any of the standard hydrocarbon based solvents like kerosene or Heptane when in this tubing configuration. Also, as before   DO NOT   allow Phosphate Ester based fluids to sit for long periods of time in the flow path.   ALWAYS   flush the system with Isopropyl Alcohol after analyz- ing a sample. In this configuration the bypass valve can either be replaced with an optional Kalrez modified valve  (RECOMMENDED ) or disabled in the software as described above.  3.3.4   Analyzing Samples with Soot Content > than 2%  Tests have shown that samples with soot content > 2% are too dark and require dilution. Either a clean base oil or heptane can be used as a diluent. Base oil is preferred as it does not result in a drastic change in sample viscosity. In both instances, the sample must be thoroughly shaken after the dilution process. This is particu- larly true if kerosene dilution is used where it is less likely that particles will remain in suspension. The dilution factor is entered prior to sample analysis into the Start Test Screen, Refer to Figure: 3-5: on page 3-20. See the Trouble Shooting section for information on other bubble causes. After the Enter button has been pressed and a sample analysis has begun, it is first initialized into the flow cell using the pump at its highest speed. During this period the software performs a series of diagnostic checks on the fluid to check the background stability etc. For example, a sample that contains an extremely high level of soot and cannot pass the AGC (Laser/Camera – Automatic Gain Control) test will not be pro- cessed and an error message will appear. It will require some dilution before it can be analyzed. During ini- tialization the bypass valve and intelligent transition detection algorithms work together in an attempt to get varying viscosity fluids into the cell in the shortest time possible. This reduces the consumption of low vis- cosity fluid and speeds up the processing and it ensures that more viscous oils are completely primed into the cell and free from flow lines caused by insufficient mixing of remaining flushing solvent. See Section 4.6.1 for instructions on how to introduce air during the flush sequence.  4.4   Equipment (Machine) Data and Sample Unit (Port) Data  When the analytical results are stored on the SpectroLNF Q200 computer, the samples are identified and stored according to the machine from which the sample was taken, and by the machine’s port if it has multi- ple sampling points. This information is fixed and only needs to be entered once. Thereafter, only the vari- able data, such as the sample date, oil added since last sample, operating hours and hours since overhaul need to be entered. 40   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Software Description  In the “Hydraulic” tab, the free water concentration in PPM (parts per million) is presented along the top row together with the previous result for that particular sample. There also is an entry that specifies the limits for the amount of water allowable for the machine’s sample port. The user can set the limit when a unit (port) is set up for a particular machine, or if the user wishes, it can be left blank. The purpose of this is to act as a visual warning to the user. The sample result will be highlighted in red text if the result equals or exceeds the value specified in the limit box. The ISO particle count codes are presented directly below the results for the water index. Sub-tabs are avail- able under the hydraulic tab to select various standards or a user defined particle count. The result columns correspond to the current result, previous result and limit. The ISO codes presented in the example figure are based on the latest 1999 ISO 4406 code for particles:- >4, >6, and >14 μm code. The main area of the screen shows size histograms for size ranges within the selected particle count tab. The results are illustrated graphically for previous and current samples for each code. The user can switch between the different codes by clicking on the relevant tab above the histogram. The numerical display for each code selected is shown in a table directly below the water index and the ISO codes. The particle counts are expressed as “Particles/100 ml” for the NAS, NAVAIR, CHA (RN), HAL and SAE AS4059 codes, but are expressed as “particles/ml” for the ISO codes as well as for the particle counts shown on the SpectroLNF Q200 Wear Summary Screen. A simple search feature is available. It is possible to search for individual characters located in certain Cus- tomer/Machine/Unit groups. This feature is particularly useful for customers who use only sample numbers and the samples are located in the “unknown” Customer/Machine and Port fields. In the selection box, select a sample number or date that you wish to search for and all the samples relevant to the criteria selected will appear in the results page. Selecting “None” will return every sample.  4.6.1.2   Wear Summary Tab  In the “Wear Summary” tab, (Figure 4-13), similar information is provided for used oils. Alternatively, in the “Wear Summary” tab, ( Figure: 4-18: on page 4-39), particle sizes are expressed as max- imum chord length. “Total Part/ml” provides the total number of particles that were counted for 1ml of fluid. The volume of oil that is actually processed is equal to the volume of the cell multiplied by the number of frames taken during a sample. The number of frames is set by the sample time (approximately 3500 for a 0.65 ml process volume). Hence, the volume processed and analyzed will never be exactly equal to 1 ml. This is why decimal numbers appear for counts of particles. “Avg and Std Diameter” These are two statistical counts using the maximum diameter measurement for the total number of particles. The average or mean diameter simply gives the average diameter of the total num- ber of particles. The standard deviation gives an indication of the spread of the results away from the mean or average. The nearer to zero, the closer that the spread of all the results will be to the mean. Wear mode statistics for particles greater than 20 μm are also provided for cutting wear, severe sliding wear, fatigue wear, non metallic particles and an unclassified category. The histogram on the Wear Summary screen shows the particle counts for given size ranges (green bars) compared to the previous result (blue bars).  NOTE:  These statistics are also given for maximum diameter for the individual wear particle categories in this section.
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   41 Software Description  A simple search feature is available. It is possible to search for individual characters located in certain Cus- tomer/Machine/Unit groups. This feature is particularly useful for customers who use only sample numbers and the samples are located in the “unknown” Customer/Machine and Port fields. Selecting “None” will return every sample.  4.6.1.3   Other Tabs  To the right of the “Hydraulic” and ‘Wear Summary” tabs are additional tabs to select views that are filtered according to types of wear particles. The tabs can be selected to view statistics and histograms for all wear particles, or particles interpreted by SpectroLNF Q200’s algorithms as: •   Cutting wear •   Sliding wear •   Fatigue wear and •   Nonmetallic.  4.6.1.4   Viscosity Tab  The “Viscosity” tab page contains the measured viscosity value in cP and cSt (if a fluid density was entered). On the bottom of the default main results screen, the operator is asked to declare this sample valid by press- ing “Mark Valid / Cont” or invalid by pressing “Mark Invalid / Cont”. Both valid and invalid records are saved in the database. However, only valid records are used for trending and machine diagnosis. Some of the reasons to mark a sample invalid would be if the sample bottle had run out of fluid during processing or if the operator had determined that fluid was not flowing properly through the inlet tube. After marking the sample analysis as either valid or invalid, the operator is prompted through a series of screens, starting with Figure 4-14, to flush the system and prepare it for the next sample.  4.6.2   Flush Cycle Description  A detailed description of the flush cycle follows:  Figure: 4-20: Flush of Sample Lines After Sample Analysis
42   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Software Description  1.   Place the sipper tube into a clean solvent container and press the “Continue’ button to start the flushing sequence. The flushing process utilizes the bypass valve and intelligent logic to efficiently flush the cell in the quickest time possible. The flushing time will depend on the cleanliness of the last sample and the viscosity of the oil currently in the system. The flushing time will vary from between 20 seconds to over a minute depending on the samples viscosity and the contamination level. Introducing air bubbles into the solvent stream after a transition has been found will remove considerably more particle contamination than just a single stream of solvent. Also, the introduction of air bubbles during the flushing sequence will help make the initialization period for the next sample as short as it needs to be. 2.   To introduce bubbles into the flow stream during the flushing sequence, lift the sipper in and out of the solvent bottle a few times. The bubbles can be seen to travel much quicker during or after a transition is detected. The amount of time spent flushing after a transition is solely dependant on the cleanliness of the last sample analyzed. 3.   If greater than 1 million (total) particles were recorded in a sample the message ( Figure: 4-21: on page 4-42) appears after the flush is complete. The excess contamination produced by these kinds of samples may require some additional flushing. 4.   The cell video can be viewed and examined for cleanliness and a further rinse can be employed until the cell looks clean. A beep in the software during the flush sequence indicates a transition has been found and the extra time to the end of the flush will depend on how contaminated the last sample was. During this additional flush period is a good time to introduce bubbles into the system. Clicking “Continue” displays the sample entry screen.  NOTE:  If an error was generated by the software during either the initialization or processing of a sam- ple, and the system did not produce a result, then a longer fixed flush sequence is employed at the flush point.  Figure: 4-21: Error Message for Unsatisfactory Flush
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   43 Software Description  Bubbles are generated automatically with a solenoid valve when the ASP (Automatic Sample Processor) is used in conjunction with the SpectroLNF Q200. If, during a flush sequence no transition is detected after an extended period of time, then the following error message is displayed. The most likely cause of this error state is either: •   A fluid that is too viscous •   A flow problem that is caused by the pump or •   A total blockage in the system. The trending screen permits the user to trend any of the specific abnormal wear particle types, i.e., severe, fatigue, cutting and nonmetallic. The Trending/Diagnosis screen must be setup by the user and can be customized to show particle size ranges according to wear particle types. To setup the Trend/Diagnosis screen, select “Trend/Diagnosis” from the main. The screen that appears will be blank. In order to customize the trends, select “Display/Selection” and  Figure: 4-22: Error Message for Failed Flush

The example in Figure: 4-24: on page 4-45 shows sample number 30 highlighted in that manner.  4.6.4   Wear Images Screen  The composite image viewer screen or “map’ screen, shows pixilated images of all the abnormal wear parti- cles which were found in the sample that are greater than 20 μm in maximum chord length.
