48   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Software Description  4.7   Miscellaneous Functions  This section describes the functionality of the buttons accessible from the Main Menu after the user has logged in ( Figure: 4-3: on page 4-28).  NOTE:  The SpectroLNF Q200 analyzes the outline shapes of particles, that is, their” silhouettes”. The results are generated by computing the outlines into a complex Neural-Net pattern recognition algorithm that has been trained using actual known wear particle silhouettes. Since the optical system within SpectroLNF Q200 uses transmitted light (back lighting),  it is not possible for SpectroLNF Q200- to distinguish particle color, texture or surface attributes.   Sometimes these are important attributes to be con- sidered when making an important diagnosis. Hence, the results obtained for each wear category are only those typical of that type of particle when viewed as a silhouette. This is analogous to looking at particles under the optical microscope using transmitted light. Spectro recommends that if the size or quantity of particles in the abnormal wear particle categories (severe, fatigue or cutting) increase over a period of time, a microscopic examination should be carried out to validate the particle classifications made by the SpectroLNF Q200 Ferrography (traditional Ferrography or Rotary Particle Deposition) or membrane filtration (filtergram) are also possible follow-up tech- niques. It should also be mentioned that other types of particles such as molybdenum disulfide, carbon flakes, seal material, and dark metallo- oxides, should they be present in a sample, will be classified in one of the wear categories of severe, fatigue or cutting depending upon their shape. This is because these particles block light and thus present a solid silhouette that the shape recognition software will categorize as one of the solid particle types, that is, as sliding, fatigue or cutting. Par- ticles that do not look like any of the standard categories will be placed into a category called “Other” (Unclassified). An experienced analyst uses both shape and surface features to ascer- tain wear mode based on particles present in a wear debris oil sample. However, as powerful as manual microscopic examination is, this man- ual method of particle analysis will always be subjective, “brain tax- ing” and time consuming, especially if many samples need to be analyzed. The SpectroLNF Q200-offers the advantage of fast, repeat- able and objective analysis.
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   49 Software Description  4.7.1   System Self Test  The “System Self-Test” button automatically initiates SpectroLNF Q200’s full series of internal self-tests. These are the same tests run during system start-up. These tests include: •   Test if laser / camera combination is working •   Test if laser / camera can properly determine the “Gain” setting. Failure of any self-test is recorded in the Error Log. Any course of action or recommended remedy is dis- played in a yellow dialog box when an error condition is encountered.  4.7.2   Test System / Set Screen  Selection of the “Test System / Set Screen” function on the Main Menu provides the user with the capability to verify system operation with an ISO Medium Test Dust Fluid (ISO MTD). To accomplish this, a check fluid with a known particle size is required. The necessary test fluid is ISO Medium Test Dust @ 2.8 mg/L. The certified particle size histogram characteristics of this check fluid are stored in the SpectroLNF Q200 and used to verify system performance. Before the system test can be started: 1.   Prepare the check fluid by shaking it for AT LEAST 30 seconds and degas it in an ultrasonic bath for 30-45 seconds. 2.   Insert the sipper into the sample bottle 3.   Select the “Test System With ISO MTD Fluid” button ( Figure: 4-30: on page 4-49).  NOTE:  Do not pour the ISO MTD Fluid into a sample bottle. The test must be run from the fluid out of the original manufacturer’s supplied bottle.  Figure: 4-30: System Test with ISO MTD
50   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Software Description  The sample is then drawn into the flow cell (Figure 4-25), and processed for approximately 3.5 minutes ( Figure: 4-31: on page 4-50).  Figure: 4-31: Priming Sample to Flow Cell  Figure: 4-32: Analyzing Reference Fluid
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   51 Software Description  The MTD analysis screen matches the cumulative measured results against the expected results ( Figure: 4- 33: on page 4-51). The measured data (blue trace) must be within the limits (green lines) of the reference sample (red trace). The table to the right of the graph lists the actual measured results against the reference. Refer to Appendix 5, on page 5-100 for troubleshooting hints for instances when the measured data is not within the limits (green lines).  NOTE:  SpectroLNF Q200 will not permit further samples to be processed until an acceptable result is obtained using the calibration fluid. If an unac- ceptable discrepancy is found between the nominal values and the measured values, the unit will save the result in the calibration log and display a message that recommends that calibration be run again. If it consistently fails, contact your local Spectro service representative.  NOTE:  It is possible to override the error message in case of an operator error such as using an incorrect test fluid. To clear the event and to use the system, the check box for “test with ISO MTD Required” needs to be unchecked in the Flow Cell/Tubing tab of the configuration settings, see “Flow Cell/Tubing Tab” on page 4-56 and Figure: 4-40: on page 4-57.  Figure: 4-33: Calibration Validation Results

July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   53 Software Description  4.8.1   Export Control Tab  Selection of the “Export Control” tab facilitates configuration of the SpectroLNF Q200 to export data to a local or remote location ( Figure: 4-36: on page 4-53). The selections provide the user with alternatives as to where the SpectroLNF Q 200   will write a copy of the sample analysis results. The destination file name can be entered into the Shared Dir Name field or selected by clicking on the Browser button. Required names are the workgroup name, computer name, and sharable directory name. The type of file for export to the Shared Directory location can be ether a ICAS (14 MED File with hand- shaking) or a comma delimited text file and can be selected by clicking on the ICAS or Text radio buttons. Wear images can be omitted from the exported sample data, or they can be attached and exported in several formats. By selecting “Disable Export of Wear Image Files with Sample Data”, the wear image will not be exported with the sample data. Alternatively, by selecting any of the three other options, images are attached to the sample data as, BMP, JPEG or Compressed files. Sample Export Control can be either immediate or to a que for later export. In order to spool data, the “Enable Automatic Queuing Results Export” check box must be checked. By also checking the “Enable Immediate Export to Network” box, information can be exported automatically to an external PC or drive each time a sample is analyzed and after the “Mark Valid / Cont” button on the Main Results Screen is pressed. This eliminates the need to go through the spooling sequence in the maintenance screen. Appendix B explains the file format in detail. It is possible to export either individual samples or all samples from a particular machine or sample port by pressing the “Export” button on the appropriate Main Results screen. These buttons permit the export of selected samples. Each time one of these buttons is pressed the relevant data is spooled internally and can be exported at any time by clicking on the “Data Maintenance” button on the Main Menu. The “Data Available”  Figure: 4-36: Export Control Tab
54   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Software Description  bar on the Data Base Maintenance Options screen ( Figure: 4-37: on page 4-54) will be green when there is data available for export. The user can export the data file to the Shared Directory on either the PC’s floppy drive, internal hard drive or to another networked PC. It is also possible to rename a file or save it to another location by selecting Other and clicking on Save Export File to bring up the browser. The “Export Specifications” selections can be accessed with a password and provide the capability to export data in three different formats as follows: 1.   Enable Legacy Export Data – This selection exports all data fields in the format selected under “File Export Type”. 2.   Enable Export Files with Unique Names – With this option selected, samples will have individual file names based on sample number, date, and time of the analysis. The format is shown in the following example: 024_0909_0845.txt  NOTE:  If a sample number is not entered, the sample number field will be automatically replaced with “unk” to signify that it is “unknown”.  Figure: 4-37: Database Maintenance Tab
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   55 Software Description  3.   Export Data List – This selection allows the user to select from all the fields which ones to export with data. When “Export Data List” is selected, the screen shown in Figure: 4-38: on page 4-55 appears. The column to the left in Figure: 4-38: on page 4-55 shows all the available fields, and the column on the right, those selected by the user to export. Fields are selected by: 1.   Clicking on the name in the left column to highlight it 2.   Click on the top “>” button. To select all fields, click the double “>>” button. 56   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Software Description  4.8.2   Miscellaneous Tab  Selecting the “Misc” (Miscellaneous) tab allows access to setting the system clock, loading software updates, selecting printer drivers and setting user defined particle size fields, ( Figure: 4-39: on page 4-56). Clock operation is maintained even with line power removed, and should not require adjustment.   The clock format is also automatically set based on registry entry for each system.   Another selection allows software upgrades of the SpectroLNF Q200 to be loaded by the user. A series of screens prompt the user to backup their database and then insert the software update diskettes. The type of printer may also be selected for con- nection to the standard printer port on the back of the unit. The Windows 2000 and Windows XP operating system contains drivers that support all major printer manufacturers. A total of six fields are available to setup user defined particle size reporting. Any size range between 4 and 255 microns can be entered into the fields. Press the “Save Entries” button and the results for up to six user defined size ranges will be available and reported by selecting the “User Defined” tab on the hydraulic fluid main results screen ( Figure: 4-16: on page 4-38).  4.8.3   Flow Cell/Tubing Tab  The entry of flow cell data is required whenever a new flow cell is replaced in the SpectroLNF Q200 and is accessible by selecting the “Flow Cell/Tubing” tab. The top area of this screen ( Figure: 4-40: on page 4-57) facilitates this data entry. Flow cells have slight variations in spacing of the parallel glass plates through which the fluid is pumped. Measuring the volume of oil analyzed is dependent upon an accurate knowledge of flow cell thickness. Hence, this option allows entry of a normalization factor to account for any manufacturing variance between the flow cells. The required information is supplied with the replacement flow cell. This screen also has a “Reset Hours” button (A) that should be pressed when new tubing is installed so that the tubing life warning message will not occur for another 200 hours. The “Test Reminder Interval” button allows the definition of the interval at which the unit requests that an ISO MTD (check fluid) test verification  Figure: 4-39: Miscellaneous Tab
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   57 Software Description  be performed. The test reminder can be switched off permanently by un-checking the “Test with ISO MTD” required box on the screen.  4.8.4   Sample Processing Tab  Selection of the “Sample Processing” tab provides the user with the options shown in Figure: 4-41: on page 4-57. Each available option will be explained below.  4.8.4.1   Enable Ultrasound and Shake Time  This option is not used for the SpectroLNF Q200. It is a tab used for the earlier LNF-M that had a built-in ultrasonic bath. This box should always be left unchecked.  Figure: 4-40: Flow Cell Tubing Tab  A  Figure: 4-41: Sample Processing Tab
58   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Software Description 4.8.4.2   Enable Entering Only Sample Number and Not Machine Info  Check this box if the SpectroLNF Q200 database will not be used. This would be the case for a lab in which SpectroLNF Q200 data is exported to another database or LIMS (Laboratory Information management Sys- tem) such that the database included in the SpectroLNF Q200-software would not be needed.  4.8.4.3   Enable Automatic Extended Processing of Clean Samples (< 96 c/ml)  Checking this box automatically extends the processing time for samples with less than 96 particles/ml. Many users choose not to extend processing time for clean samples on the belief that determining precise particle counts for clean samples provides no additional diagnostic benefit. If a sample is clean, does it matter how clean? Therefore, most users leave this box unchecked.  4.8.4.4   Fluid Volume Sampled  The SpectroLNF Q200 default processing is 2.2 minutes, which has been found to give good statistics for a relatively small sample volume and short sample period. Before processing a sample, it is possible to change the processing time. The longer a sample is processed the better the counting statistics will be, especially for the larger particles which occur in small numbers in relatively clean oil and hydraulic samples. The operator has the choice between times ranging from 1 minute up to 12.1 minutes. The longer times will obviously require more sample than the shorter periods This screen allows the sample processing interval to be set by selecting either 0.3, 0.65, 1.00, 2.00 or 4.00 ml of fluid to be processed. The SpectroLNF Q200 default setting is set 0.65 ml, this gives a relatively quick sample processing time with good counting statistics.  4.8.4.5   Flushing, Max Part/ml after Flush  At the end of the flush cycle the SpectroLNF Q200 checks for cleanliness of the flushing solvent (kerosene is recommended). The default setting is 500 particles/ml. Flushing continues until the measured concentration is less than 500 particles/ml or until the maximum flush time of 3.5 minutes is reached. If the maximum flush time is reached an error message will be displayed. The operator may adjust the solvent cleanliness value as desired.  4.8.4.6   Enable Flowcell Bypass  In normal operation the flow cell bypass will be enabled. This causes the bypass valve to open at the begin- ning of the flush cycle so that sample oil remaining in the tubing between the sample bottle and the flow cell passes through the valve instead of flowing through the cell. This provides a path of much lower resistance than forcing the oil through the flow cell. Consequently, the remaining oil is pumped out of the SpectroLNF Q200 faster than would otherwise be the case.  4.8.4.7   Auto Sampler  The auto sampler box is checked only if an ASP (Automatic Sample Processor) is being used in conjunction with the SpectroLNF Q200.  4.8.4.8   Pump PWM Control  This box allows control of the pump speed and would normally be left in it’s default settings of 500 ms cycle time and 20% duty cycle.
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   59 Software Description 4.8.4.9   NAS 1638/NAVAIR Legacy  Enabling this function by checking the box causes the display of these two codes to revert to the way they were calculated before software version 4.1. This is not recommended for routine use so that the SpectroLNF Q200 data correlates with optical particle counters.  4.8.5   Soot Tab  The ability of the SpectroLNF Q200 to measure soot in diesel engine samples became available with soft- ware version 3.97. The SpectroLNF Q200 soot calculation is similar to, and correlates to JOAP approved FT- IR spectrometer reporting soot in percentages. The SpectroLNF Q200 calculates transmittance from absor- bance through the sample from which a calibrated soot percentage is derived and can report accurately down to 0.01% (± 0.005%). The soot baseline parameters need to be configured periodically to compensate for drift over time. 1.   Select the “Soot” tab on the configuration settings screen and the screen shown in Figure: 4-42: on page 4-59 appears. 2.   Enter the password (11 is the default) if not done previously. 3.   Click on “Establish Baseline” and a reminder to prepare a clean flushing sample for analy- sis appears ( Figure: 4-43: on page 4-60). 4.   Click on “Calc Baseline”  NOTE:  The soot baseline must be set the first time the SpectroLNF Q200 is turned on, or whenever the complete operating software is installed. See also Section 5.7.  Figure: 4-42: Soot Normalization Screen
60   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Software Description  The screen shown in Figure: 4-44: on page 4-60 appears. 5.   Wipe the outside of the inlet tube and insert it into the solvent bottle. A clean solvent such as kerosene or a base oil can be used for this purpose. The bottle should contain at least 50 ml. Clean solvents or base oils without any soot will have a transmission of 100%.  Figure: 4-43: Warning Screen  Figure: 4-44: Soot Solvent/Base Oil Screen
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   61 Software Description  6.   Click on “Continue” and the screen shown in Figure: 4-45: on page 4-61 appears. 7.   Click “Continue” again if the solvent or base oil are ready to be pumped into the Spec- troLNF Q200 to start the baseline process. The screen shown in Figure: 4-46: on page 4-61 will appear to indicate that the process has started.  Figure: 4-45: Ready to Start Soot Baseline Screen  Figure: 4-46: Flushing System Screen
62   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Software Description  The SpectroLNF Q200 then calculates the optical properties of your system, Figure: 4-47: on page 4-62, so absorbance can be normalized to known values. When the process is complete, a pass or fail message appears. 8.   Repeat the process if it failed or click on the “Save Entries” button, Figure: 4-48: on page 4-62, after it passes. It is possible to check the drift over time by going back to this screen and by selecting “Test Baseline” at any time. The SpectroLNF Q200 also tests the baseline automatically each time it is started and will provide a warning if a substantial absorbance has been calculated with a supposed clean cell. If the warning message appears, thoroughly flush the system and repeat the baseline test. 76   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Software Description  4.12   Dynamic Alarm Limits  This section provides a background definition of dynamic equilibrium and how it is used to set alarm limits for SpectroLNF Q200 data. Limits can be set for the particle count specifications and for wear trends.  4.12.1   Introduction  It has been demonstrated that particles reach a dynamic equilibrium level in normal operating machines. This occurs because particles are generated at a constant rate and are then removed in the system by certain mech- anisms such as filters or settling. An equilibrium level is reached when the removal rate equals the produc- tion rate. In order to determine alarm limits for the SpectroLNF Q200, a dynamic equilibrium state needs to be estab- lished based on a minimum of five samples. The effect of an oil change must be considered because of the time it takes to regain the equilibrium particle concentration. The characteristic operating time it takes to return to dynamic equilibrium varies with the machine from which samples are taken. Factors which influ- ence operating time to reach equilibrium are: •   Filtration - The better the filter the shorter the time to equilibrium. •   Oil Cycle Rate - The faster the oil cycle rate the quicker the time to equilibrium, some lubricant systems circulate the entire oil volume as much as 5 times per minute, while others may only circulate it once per hour. •   Particle Size Being Tracked - The larger the particle size range being trended the quicker it will take to reach equilibrium. Large particles are more easily captured by filters than small particles.  4.12.2   How SpectroLNF Q200 Calculates Alarm Limits  The statistical limit is established by the SpectroLNF Q200 software by calculating the standard deviation (sigma [s]) of the sample range in the selected trend, and recorded as a limit above the mean. The production of wear particles as measured by the SpectroLNF Q200 is a normal distribution, i.e., the concentration is directly proportional to wear rate. Multiples of sigma can thus be used to establish the limit based upon how loose or tight it is desired to have the alarm set.  NOTE:  Systems like gearboxes that do not circulate the lubricant reach an equilibrium state principally due to settling and comminution (grinding up of particles). The time to achieve equilibrium in gear systems is long compared to circulating systems.  NOTE:  Since large particles are so important to incipient failure detection by SpectroLNF Q200, every effort should be made to take samples before an in-line filter.
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   77 Software Description  The multiple of sigma determines the confidence factor on the number of particles that fall within the mean and the upper alarm limit. •   A one sigma value provides a 68.7% confidence factor that all the particles above this limit are due to a imminent problem and not due to normal wear. •   A two sigma value provides a 95.4% confidence factor and •   A three sigma value a 99.7% confidence factor. The standard deviation multiple used depends on the machine being monitored and the desired confidence factor.  4.12.3   Setting Alarm Limits for Particle Count Standards and Wear Summaries  Alarm limits are linked to a machine’s port from which the oil sample was taken, therefore the steps to set an alarm limit start with the Unit Record. July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   83  5  Operation/Maintenance  5.1   Overview  Corrective/unscheduled maintenance on the SpectroLNF Q200 is limited to removal and replacement of the flow cell, tubing in the peristaltic pump head and the replacement and/or cleaning of the filter located in the sipper tube assembly. Spare parts are available from Spectro Incorporated. The service department should be contacted for assistance with all other failures or malfunctions.  5.2   Peristaltic Pump Tubing and Sipper Output Tubing Replacement  The peristaltic pump tubing is made of Viton and has an operational life of approximately 200 hours. The SpectroLNF Q200 software measures the total time that has accumulated while the pump head is turning and notifies the operator prior to a new sample run if the recommended operational life of the tubing has been exceeded. The sipper output tube is also made of Viton, however, it is transparent so that it is possible to verify that there is sample flowing during an analysis or during the flush cycle. The sipper output tube should be replaced after approximately 400 hours of use, or every other time that the peristaltic pump tube is replaced. To change the tubing out of the peristaltic pump head: 1.   Shut down the unit and unplug the power cord. 2.   Detach the tubing from the unit by pulling on the quick release latch located on the pump (pull in a direction away, toward the side of the unit) and lift upward releasing the pressure plate from the rotor. 3.   Insert new tubing and perform the preceding steps in reverse. Disengage the peristaltic pump pressure clamps when the SpectroLNF Q200 is not in use. This will prevent the tubing from becoming deformed and possibly restricting sample flow. Release the spring-loaded tubing clamps, by pushing in on the quick release levers. Next, pull out the nozzles from the clamps. Remove the peristaltic pump tubing from the nozzles and replace with a similar length of tubing. Remove the sipper output tube from the nozzle and cut the nylon cable tie that secures the other end of the tube to the top of the sipper assembly. Replace the sipper output tubing by reversing the procedure. CAUTION: Disengage the peristaltic pump pressure clamps when the SpectroLNF Q200 is not in use. This will prevent the tubing from becoming deformed and possibly restricting sample flow.  NOTE:  The peristaltic pump tube can be conserved by simply moving it to a fresh position on the tube, rather than replacing the entire tube.

A flow cell that is installed incorrectly will have a black band near the bottom of the image, In most cases, there is sufficient play in the screws holding the flow cell to rearrange it until the black band disappears. If not, remove the flow cell and reverse the input and output connections.  5.5   Flow Cell Cleaning Procedures  When contamination is inside the flow cell, it must be cleaned and removed in order to maintain the Spec- troLNF Q200’s performance. The flow cell cleaning procedure is non-invasive and consists of a manual back flush and a manual forward flush. With this process, the SpectroLNF Q200 does not need to be opened to gain access to the flow cell for cleaning. In this procedure, the cell is flushed in both flow directions by pumping clean relatively viscous oil such as a 10W40 or 75 cSt base oil through the flow cell. This procedure should be performed after consistent “flow line” error messages are seen. WARNING: The flow cell reference ledge is made of glass. The flow cell must rest squarely in the shallow recess of the retaining block or the flow cell will be broken when the retaining block screws are tightened even though a mechanical stop prevents the flow cell from being overtight- ened. The main screen re-appears.  5.5.2   Forward Flush  For the forward flush, the SpectroLNF Q200 is setup in the normal operating configuration for sample input and output. Remove the peristaltic pump tube from the container with the clean oil and place the tube back into a waste container. Remove and disconnect the temporary input tube from the SpectroLNF Q200 bulk- head. Reattach the sipper tube connector to the SpectroLNF Q200 bulkhead. Insert the sipper tube into the container with the oil that was removed from the peristaltic pump end during the back flush. To perform a forward flush: 1.   On the main screen, select “Flush / Display”. 2.   Select “Manual Forward Flush” on the next screen. 3.   Select “Display Image” on the next screen. 4.   Select “Display” to view a live image of the fluid in the flow cell. 5.   Observe the fluid flow through the cell. There should be a brief transition when the oil enters the cell (bubbles and flow lines) followed by a stabilized flow through the cell free of flow lines and contamination. 6.   If the flow in step 5 is clear, it is possible to analyze a test fluid a few times to check for repeatability and the absence of error messages. 7.   If the results are acceptable, proceed with normal sample analysis. Although this procedure should clean any stuck particles from the flow cell, it may be necessary to repeat the cleaning and back flush procedures 3 or 4 times until the flow cell is completely clean.  5.6   Operational Validation Test (ISO MTD Fluid)  The SpectroLNF Q200 system operation can be verified by analyzing a check fluid that contains known quantities of particles and particle sizes. The next two subsections describe the procedure for analyzing an ISO MTD Fluid and provides examples of normal data (see “Operational Validation Test (ISO MTD Fluid) – Normal Results” on page 5-101) and abnormal data (see “Operational Validation Test (ISO MTD Fluid) –  Figure: 5-32: Select “Display Image” (A)  A
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   101 Operation/Maintenance  Abnormal Results” on page 5-103). Where appropriate, the abnormal data also includes recommendations for troubleshooting and corrective action.  5.6.1   Operational Validation Test (ISO MTD Fluid) – Normal Results  This function is also described in the Software Description section 4.7.2. Since it is also a maintenance func- tion, it is repeated here for the convenience of the user. Selection of the “Test System” function from the Main Menu provides the user with the capability to verify system operation with an ISO Medium Test Dust Fluid (ISO MTD). To accomplish this, a check fluid with a known particle size is required. The necessary test fluid is ISO Medium Test Dust @ 2.8 mg/L. The certified particle size histogram characteristics of this check fluid are stored in the SpectroLNF Q200 software and are used to verify system performance. Before the system test can be started: 1.   Prepare the check fluid by shaking it for AT LEAST 30 seconds and then degassing it in an ultra- sonic bath for 30-45 seconds. 2.   Insert the sipper into the sample bottle. 3.   Select “Particle Count Check” button (A)). The check fluid is then:  Figure: 5-33:  A
102   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Operation/Maintenance  1.   Drawn into the flow cell, 2.   Processed for approximately 9 minutes,  Figure: 5-34: Priming Sample to Flow Cell  Figure: 5-35: Analyzing Reference Fluid
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   103 Operation/Maintenance  3.   The MTD analysis screen matches the cumulative measured results against the expected results. The measured data (blue trace) must be within the limits (green lines) of the reference sample (red trace). The table to the right of the graph lists the actual measured results against the reference. SpectroLNF Q200 will not permit further samples to be processed until an acceptable result is obtained using the calibration fluid. If an unacceptable discrepancy is found between the nominal values and the measured values, the unit will save the result in the calibration log and display a message that recommends that calibra- tion be run again. If it fails a second time and for the same reason, compare the results to the sample curves for additional troubleshooting  5.6.2   Operational Validation Test (ISO MTD Fluid) – Abnormal Results  The SpectroLNF Q200 does not require periodic calibration because it is “intrinsically correct”, that is, the count is based on the actual images of the particles. The SpectroLNF Q200 optics are set at the factory and periodic calibration is not required. If calibration validation results (see “Operational Validation Test (ISO MTD Fluid)” on page 5-100) fall outside of the minimum or maximum limits, it could be due to problems in the sample flow system or due to damage resulting from a severe physical shock. Pump not turning. Check for obstructions and try flush again. Or Pump not turning, check if pump head is bind- ing. 110   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Operation/Maintenance  During the initialization the laser and camera work together to try and adjust the background intensity to a set value. This value is arrived at by adjusting the gain of the camera and the pulse width of the laser to obtain the desired average intensity of each gray scale pixel in the image.   The laser remains at a constant intensity but the pulse width changes based on the fluid darkness. The pulse width varies between 1 microsecond and 300 microseconds. The error screen below appears if the fluid is too dark and the camera gain and laser pulse width are at their maximum settings such that the background cannot be brightened any more. Call for service if the problem persists and the error message does not go away.  5.8.2   Fluid Too Clean  Once the camera/laser settings have been established, the flow is checked by processing 200 frames of data. During this trial process time, the SpectroLNF Q200 looks for at least 1 particle in 200 frames (approxi- mately 30 particles/ml), but excluding flow lines or bubbles. If this criterion is not reached, then the software will give an error, Figure 5-38, to indicate possible causes such as fluid being too clean, fluid being too vis- cous or the pump not being engaged. The steady state intensity value of the background image is monitored for stability over a period of time. If the steady state intensity value of the background varies beyond set limits, flow lines are most likely the cause. More fluid will need to be ini- tialized and flushed through the cell in an attempt to clean it. Frames may be rejected during the process time if one of the following occurs: •   Over 2,000 particles exist in a single frame •   Excessive flow lines are detected •   Dividing by zero or other arithmetic errors in the shape recognition algorithms •   Too many objects for the interface to handle  Area to Check   Possible Problem   Remedy  Fluid Viscos- ity Sample is too viscous i.e. >ISO 150 grade oil. Viscous oils take longer to eliminate flow lines. Dilute sample with solvent, see Section 3.2 and Appendix A. Seals and T Fitting Air bubbles induced from leaking seals.   Check that sipper head and bypass connector T fittings are not leaking any air into the sys- tem. Flow Cell   Leaking causing large bubble intake.   Check cell for leaks – call for service – replace cell. Flow Cell   Obstructed   Remove inlet filter and perform Flush/Back Flush. Fluid   High levels of undissolved water/coolant can cause large fluid instabilities. Heat sample to boil off excess water so sample can be analyzed.
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   113 Operation/Maintenance  If 60% of the total frames are thrown away at any one point during the process period the SpectroLNF Q200 will error out, Figure 5-40. To correct for the error, the user will have the option to run the sample again or try to back flush the sample. It is also possible to display the video to further confirm the problem. If the problem is clearly caused by too many objects, then a dilution of the sample can be performed to correct it. This error state is also seen frequently with samples that contain heavy water contamination and emulsions. The error is most probably sample related, but also check the following table If the problem persists with all types of samples, call service.  Table 5-50:  Area to Check   Possible Problem   Remedy  Flow Cell   Obstructed   Remove inlet filter and perform Flush/Back- flush.  Figure: 5-49: Too Many Frames Rejected
114   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Operation/Maintenance  5.9.2   Stuck Particles/Flow Lines  During the end of the sample processing period, the software looks for particles that have not moved and have appeared in the same location all of the frames. When this condition occurs, it is displayed as an error message. The user then has the option to view the data manually to evaluate and verify the error condition. The coordi- nate of the location of a stuck particle is also displayed as a popup message box. Coordinate 0,0 is the top left and 640,480 is the bottom right. The corrective action for a stuck particle is to view the cell video using the software to confirm that the parti- cle or fiber still remains at one location using the provided row and column coordinates. If the particle does remain in one location, remove the inlet filter and perform a back flush to try and remove the object. If the particle still remains after a series of attempts at back flushing, the flow cell cleaning procedure, Section 5.1.2 should be performed. Stuck particles upstream of the cell can also be a cause of flow lines. In this case, just a single row number will be visible on the message screen. Flow lines are a product of unstable fluid flow and stuck particles. This can cause the intensity of the image to fluctuate considerably, leading to inaccurate results. To eliminate the error message, the particles will have to be removed by performing the flow cell cleaning as described in Section 5.1.2.  5.9.3   Extended Processing  When a sample is very clean and contains only few small (4-8 micron) particles, the process time of the SpectroLNF Q200 is automatically extended by a calculated amount so that a better statistical result can be obtained. This occurs only if the extended processing feature of the SpectroLNF Q200 is enabled in the con- figuration screen, Figure 4-34. It will extend the process time of a sample if <450 particles in the 4-8 micron  Figure: 5-51: Stuck Particle Error Message
July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   115 Operation/Maintenance  particle range are seen in the volume of fluid selected. The message for this condition is shown in Figure 5- 42. The extended process time is variable for each sample and it is calculated based on the number of particles counted over the set volume. The maximum extended time is reached when the volume analyzed reaches 1 ml at which time the system will time out.  5.10   Data Backup  Starting with SpectroLNF Q200 software version 3.97, the system automatically performs daily, monthly and yearly backups each time the system is restarted. The daily backup is performed automatically each day the system is restarted. The monthly backup includes the wear particle image data (wearpart.bin files) and the user will be prompted to save this backup to another PC or removable media. The following message prompts the user the first time the system is restarted in a new month. The backup data is stored in  C:\programfiles\lockheedMartin\lasernetfines\Archive. July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   119  6  Automatic Sample Processor (ASP) for LaserNet Fines   ®  6.1   Description  The ASP, is a self-contained sample changer and processor designed specifically for use with SpectroLNF Q200. It provides the user with automatic and unattended processing of 24 used oil or hydraulic fluid sam- ples. The key ASP features are: •   Specifically designed to work with the SpectroLNF Q200. •   Two-stage washing system using spray jets, •   Automatic reversing sample stirrer, •   Artificial intelligence monitors for complete process routine, •   Quick disconnect/shutoff for solvent supply and waste drain lines, •   Uses inexpensive solvents.  6.1.1   ASP Operation  The ASP features a reversing paddle to stir the sample and to provide a homogeneous particle distribution. 1.   While the SpectroLNF Q200 analyzes a sample, the ASP stirs the next one.  Figure: 6-1: Automatic Sample Processor (ASP)
120   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Automatic Sample Processor (ASP) for  2.   A peristaltic pump draws the sample through the sipper of the ASP to the SpectroLNF Q200. 3.   After processing, a robot elevates the sample sipper and sample stirrer from their respec- tive sample bottles and lowers them into spray chambers for cleaning. 4.   Rinse solvent, interspersed with air gaps, is also pumped through the sipper, SpectroLNF Q200 tubing and the SpectroLNF Q200 flow cell to wash away traces of the previous sam- ple. 5.   The procedure is repeated until up to 24 samples are analyzed and processed. 6.   Sensors monitor the complete process, step by step, and shut down the system if an error occurs.  6.1.2   ASP Specifications  CAUTION: Safety and performance of the ASP will be impaired if it is not used in the manner stated and intended in this manual. July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   125 Automatic Sample Processor (ASP) for  6.2   Connecting the ASP to the SpectroLNF Q200  This section provides detailed instructions to set-up the ASP and preparing it for the analysis of fluid sam- ples. Instructions include: •   Descriptions of the plumbing requirements for solvent and sample flow and •   Electrical connections for communications between ASP and the PC.  6.2.1   Plumbing Setup  The ASP is delivered with 10 feet (3 meters) of tubing and fittings in order to provide flexibility for the user in the placement of the solvent supply reservoir and waste drain reservoir. The user has to cut the tubing to size and attach the fittings for the solvent supply line and the drain line. The ASP solvent system diagram (Figure: 7-6: on page 7-8) and the procedure to assemble the solvent supply line and drain line follow. 126   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   July 2012/Version 5.1 Automatic Sample Processor (ASP) for 6.2.1.1   Solvent Supply Line / Filter Assembly  The ASP uses a solvent to disperse and remove the residual oil sample from the sample stirring process and the sample probe to prevent cross contamination. In general the recommended solvents are highly refined and are free from particle contamination; however during transportation, transfer or storage the fluid can become contaminated with foreign debris that could induce an analytical error or interrupt the automatic operation of the ASP. For these reasons, the ASP solvent supply line is provided with a disposable in-line fil- ter (DIF). The solvent supply line provides solvent solution from a reservoir, through a filter and to the ASP for use in cleaning the sipper tube and sample stirrer. The line is made to size by the user and consists of following parts:  Table 6-12: Solvent Supply Line Parts Part #   Description P/N LF9064   1/8 inch diameter Viton tubing cut to length to reach from the reservoir to the in-line filter. In preparation to installing the filter, install a tube stem expander fitting (P/N P-10670) on one end of a piece of the cut tubing by pushing the fitting’s stem into the tubing (Figure: 7- 7: on page 7-9). Before installing the DIF, note that it has no flow direction indicator which means the filter can be installed in either direction and it will function correctly. The cleaning bath spray jets are factory set, however this procedure also takes you through the steps to check for possible over-spray in case a slight adjustment is necessary. To prime the ASP solvent tubing and to check the solvent reservoirs for over spray: 1.   Turn ON the ASP with the main power switch in the rear of the instrument (Figure: 7-3: on page 7- 5). 2.   After a few seconds, the startup screen, Figure: 7-15: on page 7-16, will appear on the ASP control panel. The control panel is a touch screen and instructions are entered by pressing on the appropriate instruction. 3.   Press “Disable Remote” (A). Disabling the remote provides the user local control to oper- ate the ASP manually through the control panel. On the other hand, when the remote is enabled, the SpectroLNF Q200 controls the ASP with instructions in its software. 4.   Press “Perform Tasks” (A). WARNING: Under no circumstance should volatile solvents such as hexane, hep- tane, naphtha, mineral spirits, toluene or gasoline (petrol) be used in the Automatic Sample Processor (ASP) as there is a risk of fire or explosion. July 2012/Version 5.1   SpectroLNF Q200 Instruction Manual   139 Automatic Sample Processor (ASP) for  A sample processing sequence diagram for the ASP functions is shown below. Total sample processing time varies from 2.5 to 5 minutes per sample. The time varies depending on sample viscosity and the fluid volume setting selected in the communications setup. When sample analysis time is important, the lowest sample volume and process time (0.30 ml and 1.1 min- utes) settings can be used to screen the samples. A more detailed analysis can then be performed if one or more of the samples are suspect. Total analysis time and solvent consumption will vary with SpectroLNF Q200 software settings and sample viscosity. The ASP can handle samples with viscosities up to 150 cSt at 40° C. All samples with viscosities above this limit will require dilutions with solvent prior to being analyzed with the ASP. Refer to Appendix B, on page B-1 for recommended dilution requirements. Section 1.0  Introduction  & System  Description  1.1   INTRODUCTION  Congratulations on your purchase of the Spectro-  Visc. By reading this manual thoroughly and fol -  lowing the guidelines described herein for use and  maintenance, you will be able to obtain the best  possible results from this advanced viscometer.  Please ensure that the equipment is used at all  times by qualified personnel. If after reading this  manual, anything remains unclear or additional  explanation is required, please contact your sup -  plier or Spectro Inc.  WARNING!!  Installation, operation or maintenance in a way  other than described within this manual may  lead to hazardous situations and may void the  manufacturer's warranty.  1.2   SYSTEM OVERVIEW  Viscosity is a measure of a fluid’s resistance to flow  at a given temperature. In the case of lubricants,  it is commonly perceived as "thickness", of the  oil, or its resistance to flow. Thus, water is "thin",  having a lower viscosity, while oil is "thick" hav -  ing a higher viscosity.  Used oil analysis laboratories, lubricant manufac -  turers and blenders rely on viscosity measurements  as a key parameter in determining the condition  of in-service oil, or specification of a new oil. For  example, changes in viscosity of used oils can be  an indication of lubricant degradation (increase  in viscosity), fuel dilution (decrease in viscosity),  or a lubricant mix-up.  The principle of operation of the Spectro-Visc is  based on the fact that an oil sample flows through  a viscometer tube under the influence of gravity.  The time it takes for the sample to flow through  a section of the tube is proportional to viscosity  as follows:  v = kt  Spectro-Visc Automatic Viscometer   |   1  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
where:  v   = viscosity in cSt  k   = tube constant in cSt/second  t   = seconds.  Light sensors measure the passage of the oil from  the beginning of the measuring section to the end  of the measuring section.   The tube is calibrated  using the Spectro-Visc at a carefully controlled  temperature with oil calibration standards, thus  determining the tube constant,   k .  After   k   is known, the time for an sample of un -  known viscosity to flow through the tube deter -  mines viscosity by multiplying   k   times   t .  The Spectro-Visc analysis procedure starts with  the user injecting less than 1 ml of sample into the  measuring tube. Before it travels to the capillary,  the sample warms up to bath temperature as it  travels down the tube and collects in its horizontal  arm. The measurement time is initiated when the  bottom of the oil column reaches the first optical  sensor. The “Busy” LED for the tube will light up  denoting that the measurement has started.   The  oil sample will continue to travel down the capil -  lary and the system will terminate the measuring  time as soon as the second optical sensor has been  reached. At this time, the kinematic viscosity re -  sult for the tube will be shown on the LCD screen  or on an optional computer or printer.  Immediately upon completion of the measure -  ment, the system automatically starts the cleaning  cycle by first draining the tube, performing the  user specified number of cleaning solvent injec -  tions and finally drying the tube.   An optional  dual cleaning solvent system is also available for  difficult and heavily contaminated samples.   The  entire cycle time from sample injection to data  readout ranges from 4 to 8 minutes per tube  when ASTM D445 precision is required. Sample  throughput can be increased considerably by re -  ducing cycle times for used oil analysis applica -  tions based on trending.  1.3   SYSTEM DESCRIPTION  The Spectro-Visc is a self-contained bench-top  automatic kinematic temperature bath viscosity  measuring system that is ideal for used oil analysis  laboratories that need to test a wide range of lubri -  cant viscosities in a relatively short analysis cycle.  Concurrently, for the analysis of new lubricants it  conforms to the precise requirements of ASTM  D445, D446, D7279, IP 71 and ISO 3104.  The Spectro-Visc consists of a thermostatic bath  with a circular heater and a control column. The  bath contains 4 patented viscometer tubes togeth -  er with optical sensors to detect the flow of oil  through the tubes. All measuring tubes function  independently of each other. The control column  has an LCD display that provides the user with  information about the system’s status and an array  of LED’s indicate the current status of each mea -  suring tube. An optional external computer can  also be used to control the system for applications  where more extensive data handling requirements  are necessary.  A description of the Spectro-Visc’s main compo -  nents, Figure 1-1, follows.  1.   Thermostat   - Used to accurately set the  temperature of the thermostatic bath. The  temperature can be changed with a simple  touch of a button.  2.   Solvent Injectors   - A nozzle for each tube  injects the solvent necessary for cleaning and  drying the viscosity tubes after each mea -  surement. All four nozzles are mounted on a  bridge which can be moved upward to facili -  tate replacement of the viscometer tubes.  3.   Viscometer Tubes   - Four tubes in which the  viscosity determination is made. The sample  is injected at the top of the tube and flows  due to gravity past two sensors and into a  drain.  4.   Thermostatic Bath   - The bath is filled with  oil that is maintained at the temperature set  on the thermostat. The viscometer tubes are  in the bath. When an oil sample is injected  into a tube, the oil reaches the bath tem -  2   |   Operation & User Maintenance Manual  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
perature before the oil reaches the measuring  section.  5.   Optical Fiber Housing   - Contains the  optical sensors required for detection of the  oil. The top sensor starts the measurement  and the bottom sensor stops it. Between  the sensors is an opening through which the  user can visually check the fluid during the  measurement.  6.   Mixing Chambers   - The chambers prevent  oil with high pour point from becoming  solid after leaving a tube and thus clogging  the system. The chambers are mounted be -  low the bath and before the drain tubes and  are kept warm due to direct contact with the  bath. During the cleaning cycle, the oil is  mixed with solvent in the chambers further  reducing the risk of clogging the drain tubes.  7 . Control Panel and LCD Screen   - Function  keys are used for configuration and interac -  tion with the instrument parameters. The  LCD screen displays instrument settings and  provides continuous feedback to the user.  8.   Status LED’s   - The LED matrix provides  constant visual feedback reflecting the status  of each tube.  9.   Leveling Eye   - Allows the user to easily level  the instrument by adjusting the leveling feet.  10.   Leveling Feet   - Provides the capability to  modify the height and horizontal alignment  of the system by turning the black grips.  7. Section 2  Site Selection &  Installation  2.1   SITE SELECTION  The Spectro-Visc is a bench-top instrument that  requires little counter space for operation. There  are several things that need to be taken into ac -  count to facilitate its operation and maintenance.  The Spectro-Visc should be placed on a level  surface which is free from vibration and located  close to the utilities required for operation such as  power, compressed air and venting. It should be  placed into a well ventilated room, or ideally un -  der a fume hood. Alternatively, it is also possible  to exhaust the instrument into a suitable vent by  removing the muffler from its 1/4 inch female fit -  ting and replacing it with a hose attached to a 1/4  inch male fitting (Section 2.3).  WARNING!!!  A fume hood or exhaust system into an air duct  should be used when toluene is used as a solvent  to clean and dry the viscometer tubes.  The   Spectro-Visc   bath   generates   a   minimal  amount of heat and some heating of the sur -  roundings will occur.   An air-conditioned room  is recommended for optimal temperature stability  of the thermostatic bath. It is also good practice  not to place the instrument in front of a window  or door where the sun or drafts may cause tem -  perature changes.  2.2   ITEMS INCLUDED WITH SPECTRO-  VISC  The Spectro-Visc is delivered with all the parts re -  quired for installation and operation.   The only  optional item are a compressor for air supply and  a printer.  The standard Spectro-Visc system includes the  following:  1. Bench-top viscometer.  2. Four viscometer tubes (constants specified  by the customer).  3. Four viscosity standards (specified by the  customer). The following is the step-by-  step installation procedure to be performed after  a suitable site as described in Section 2.1 has been  selected. Please take the time to read through and  familiarize yourself with the procedure before ac -  tually performing the functions.  1. Install the leveling feet, Figure 1-1, Items 10.  The leveling feet have been removed prior to ship -  ping and must be installed by screwing them into  their mounting holes. There are a total of 5 level -  ing feet, three in the rear of the instrument and  two in the front.  2. Level the instrument to ensure that the force  of gravity will affect the oil sample in a perfectly  vertical direction.   The level of the Spectro-Visc  is adjusted by observing the leveling eye, Figure  1-1 Item 9, and by turning the black grips on the  leveling feet. The instrument is level when the air  bubble in the leveling eye is in the exact center of  the circular mark.  3. Fill the thermostatic tank. Your system is sup -  plied with a 20 liter container of thermostatic  bath oil; the capacity of the tank is approximately  7.5 liters. Connect the power  cord of the printer to a grounded power outlet.  2.4   Optional Dual Solvent System  An optional dual solvent system is available from  Spectro Inc. for applications were it may be neces -  sary to use a second solvent to properly cle4an the  viscometer tube. This is frequently the case when  measurements are made at, or near, room tempera -  ture. At these lower temperatures, the solvent that  is used to dissolve the sample does not evaporate  fast enough. Consequently, the tube will not dry  completely and solvent residue remains inside it.  To solve this, an optional dual solvent injection  system is available to introduce an additional sol -  vent which does evaporate quickly at the operating  bath temperature.  The dual solvent system differs from the standard  single solvent system in that a second solvent bot -  tle is installed and additional software is available  to program the second solvent injection intervals  during drying time.  The solvent supply bottles are 1 liter coated flasks  which hold sufficient solvent for approximately  1,000 analyses. Overpressure is created inside the  bottles using compressed air to transport the sol -  vent to the system. Section 3  Description of  Controls  3.1   INTRODUCTION  The Spectro-Visc is made up of two parts, the  thermostatic bath and a control column.   The  temperature of the thermostatic bath is set with  a thermostat and the calibration and analytical  functions are set and viewed on the control panel.  This chapter explains how to configure the con -  trols available to the user.  To view the controls described in the next sec -  tions, turn “ON” the Spectro-Visc with the “ON/  OFF” switch located above the power cord on the  rear of the instrument, Figure 2-3.  3.2   USING THE THERMOSTAT  The thermostat is very easy to use. The standard  functions to program a thermostatic bath temper -  ature are described below.  The thermostat’s primary controls are an “ON/  OFF” switch, “PROGRAM” button and an “UP”  and a “DOWN” button, Figure 3-1. By pressing  the “PROGRAM” button, various control pa -  rameters can be selected, and they can be changed  with the “UP” and/or “DOWN” buttons.  The temperature setting (lower figure) can be eas -  ily changed by pressing the “UP/DOWN” but -  tons. Holding down the button will cause the tar -  get temperature to change with increasing speed.  Figure 3-1, Thermostat  ON/OFF  Switch  Program  Button DOWN  Button  UP Button  Temperature  LIMIT  Adjustment  Target  Temperature Actual  Temperature  Spectro-Visc Automatic Viscometer   |   11  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
CAUTION!!  The temperature LIMIT has been set at the fac -  tory to 110°C. Do NOT alter this setting as the  Spectro-Visc is not suitable for higher tempera -  tures.  The pump speed setting is dependant on the  measurement temperature. Once it is set, adjust -  ments are not necessary unless you change from a  low temperature to a high temperature. At lower  temperatures (25-50°C) it is necessary to main -  tain a high pumping speed to achieve the required  temperature stability.   The pumping speed should  be set to 4 or 5 at these temperatures.   For tem -  peratures of 100°C and above the speed can be re -  duced to 2. To alter the pumping speed, press the  “Program” button once to bring up “PUMP X”,  with X being the current speed.   Press the “Pro -  gram” button again to change the value.   Now,  modify this value using the “UP” and “DOWN”  buttons.   Release and wait for a few seconds for  the parameter to stop blinking. Pressing the “UP”  button again will return you to the target tem -  perature. Please refer to the included thermostat's  manual for information about its more advanced  features.  NOTE!!  The thermostat has been optimally configured  for use with the Spectro-Visc. Any change being  made to the advanced PID-parameters of the  thermostat is likely to result in less stability and  performance  The actual temperature reading of the thermostat  has been calibrated.   However, when analytical  precision is an issue, it is not sufficient to deter -  mine the exact temperature and temperature sta -  bility.   For this, an ASTM calibrated mercury  thermometer of sufficient resolution is provided  with your instrument. Change the target temper -  ature of the thermostat according to the reading  of this thermometer. When doing this, allow the  system sufficient time to adapt to the new target  temperature (approximately 30 minutes).  3.3   CONTROL PANEL  The control panel, Figure 3-2, is used to configure  the different settings of the Spectro-Visc and the  LCD screen displays information about the status  of each tube. 3.4.3.2   PARAMETERS-Function Key F2,  Cleaning Cycle   (Standard Single Solvent  System)  The Spectro-Visc cleaning cycle consists of time  to drain a tube, time to clean a tube, time between  cleanings, number of cleanings, and drying time.  This submenu provides access to change all these  parameters. The cleaning cycle parameters can be  set individually for each tube.  The diagram below shows the different steps of  the cleaning cycle:  A   = Initial Draining Time  B   = Injection Time of Solvent  C   = Interval between Two Injections of Solvent  D   = Number of Injections of Solvent  E   = Drying Time  A cleaning cycle with the following parameters,  A=10, B=0.4, C=3, D=10 and E=60 would result  in an initial draining time of 10 seconds, followed  by 10 injections of 0.4 seconds.   Between each  injection there will be an interval of 3 seconds to  allow the pump to drain the injected solvent. Fi -  nally, there will be a drying time of 60 seconds  during which no solvent is injected and only the  vacuum remains active to pull air through the  tube.  Note that the vacuum is active during the entire  cleaning cycle to remove oil and solvent from the  tube.  Guidelines on typical times for the above param -  eters can be found in Section 4.4.  The various cleaning parameters can be set by se -  lecting “F2 Cleaning Cycle" from the PARAM -  ETERS menu. The same letters as used above will  be used to clarify the meaning of the various sub  menu's.  A. Initial Draining Time  This time serves to allow the vacuum to drain the  sample from the tube without the injection of any  solvent. This is particularly important when high  viscosity oils are analyzed as it can take some time  before the initial oil is drained from the tube. The  first submenu after selecting “F2 Cleaning Cycle”  is for the draining time, Figure 3-9. After entering  the unit number for the tube, the current drain -  ing time will be shown. To avoid the possibility  of a tube overflowing, the draining time can be  extended. Enter a new time and press " ↵ " twice  to confirm the new time, or press the “ ↓ ” button  to navigate to the following menu without chang -  ing this value.  B. Injection Time  In this submenu, Figure 3-10, you can specify the  time the injection valve opens. This allows you to  control the volume of solvent that is injected. The  time can be set in increments of 1/10 of a second.  If you are unsure what to set it to, start with 0.3  seconds and observe the effects. If this proves to  be too long and too much solvent is injected each  time, decrease the time until the injected volume  is sufficient.   If the injected volume is too low,  increase the time. On the front of the instrument, a matrix of Status  LED’s Figure 3-22, constantly indicates the status  of all measuring tubes.   Each column of LED’s  (from top to bottom) corresponds with a measur -  ing tube: the left column corresponding with the  left measuring tube, etc. The LED's indicate each  tube's status as follows :  OK   - This indicates that the corresponding mea -  suring tube is ready to receive the sample.  busy   - As the sample passes the first detection  point, this LED will light and the OK LED will  dim.  invalid   - This LED will only be activated in the  following scenarios:  - an air bubble has been detected.  - a measurement has been completed before  the minimal measuring time (specified by the  operator) had passed. Result is invalid.  - a measurement has exceeded the maximum  allowed measuring time (specified by the  operator). Result is invalid. drain  This LED is actually a push-button switch.  When pressed, it starts a cleaning cycle for the  corresponding tube. Pushing the “Drain” button will start a cleaning  cycle and the corresponding measuring tube will  subsequently be ready for a new measurement.  NOTE!!!  Should something occur during a cleaning cycle,  pressing the "Drain" button for the tube in ques -  tion will stop the cleaning cycle. Press it again to  start a new cleaning cycle from the beginning. Accessed by pressing “F4” in the main  menu (“Measuring Status”) it provides a status  message for each station. The lines of the display  correspond with the different measuring tubes:  the upper line corresponds with the far left mea -  suring tube.   The second line corresponds with  the next measuring tube and so on.  The possible status messages are given below, with  a brief explanation:  “READY TO MEASURE”  Measuring tube is set to “Viscosity” mode (see Sec -  tion 4.3) and is ready to receive the sample. The  Status-LED should simultaneously show “OK”  “READY TO CALIBRATE”  Measuring tube is set to “Calibration” mode (see  Section 4.2) and is ready to receive the sample.  The   Status-LED   should   simultaneously   show  “OK”.  “MEAS (Y) T:XXX.XX”  Indicates that a viscosity measurement has start -  ed. “Y” gives the sequence number of the current  measurement. For example, if you have set that  3 measurements have to be performed, and the  current is the second “Y” will be “2”. “X” shows a  running timer, indicating the measurement time  already elapsed.  “CALIBRATING (Y)”  Indicates   that   a   calibration   measurement   has  started. Again, "Y" gives the sequence number of  the current measurement.  “FLOWTIME ERROR”  This indicates that the minimal measuring time  was not met or the maximum time was exceeded.  The red Status-LED “Invalid” will also be on.  The measuring result is invalid and will not be  displayed. Press “Drain” to start a cleaning cycle  and reset the station.  “AIR BUBBLE !”  During the measurement, an air bubble passing  by a light sensor has been detected.   Measuring  result is invalid and will not be displayed.   Press  “Drain” to start a cleaning cycle and reset the  measuring tube.  “T:XXX.XXX V:YYY.YYYY”  This will be shown upon completion of a success - Figure 3-23, LCD Screen  Spectro-Visc Automatic Viscometer   |   20  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
ful viscosity measurement. “X” will show the total  measuring time. “Y” will show the calculated vis -  cosity of the sample.  “REF OIL V:YYY.YYY  NR. OFMEAS: X  Cn=AA.AA OK=’E’  Co=BB.BB NOT=’ESC’”  This status message will be displayed upon com -  pletion of a successful calibration, “Y” being the  viscosity of the reference oil that was entered be -  fore the calibration. “X” is the number of mea -  surements that has been performed.   “A” gives  the value of the tube constant that was calculated  by the instrument, based upon the results of the  measurements that have just been completed.  “B” gives the value of the tube constant that is  still present in the memory of the system.   Press  “ ↵ ” on the control panel to overwrite the sys -  tem’s memory with the newly calculated constant  or press “Esc” to discard and keep the old value  intact.  “DRAINING”  This is displayed during the initial draining time  in a cleaning cycle.  ”FLUSH (X) T:ZZZs”  This is displayed when the system has started a  cleaning cycle upon completion of a measure -  ment. X represents the remaining number of in -  jections of solvent. “Z” shows the remaining time  until the next injection.  "DRYING (X)"  This is displayed upon completion of the solvent  injection. X shows the remaining drying time.  “PRESS DRAIN AGAIN”  This is displayed when the user interrupts a clean -  ing cycle. Pressing the drain button again will start  a new draining cycle.  3.7   OPTIONAL PRINTER  The optional external printer is connected to the  RS-232 port on the back of the Spectro-Visc, with  the included serial communications cable. To use  the printer, set the “External” communication,  see Section 3.4.3.6, to “Printer”.  CAUTION!!!  Do not connect the printer to the RS-232 port  while the Spectro-Visc is powered ON. First,  turn OFF the system and then connect the  printer using the cable provided. Turn ON the  printer after it is connected.  When a printer is connected, it makes a distinct  difference in the operation of the instrument.  With the printer present, the system will auto -  matically start cleaning a tube after completing  a measurement. Without the printer, the system  will simply hold after a measurement and display  the result on the LCD screen.   It will not com -  mence the cleaning cycle until the user has pressed  the 'Drain" button.  When a tube is set to perform a "single measure -  ment", the printer will simply report the result of  that measurement upon completion. However, if  a tube has been configured to perform multiple  measurements, the printer will report all individ -  ual results of all measurements upon completion  of the last measurement. Section 4  Analyzing  Samples  4.1   INTRODUCTION & PREPARATION  FOR USE  This section of the manual explains the steps that  must be performed before a sample is introduced  into the Spectro-Visc. Thereafter, detailed proce -  dures for calibration and viscosity determinations  are provided.  Before actually beginning a determination, the in -  stallation steps in Section 2.3 must be completed.  The thermostatic bath must also be at operating  temperature.   It is also important   to regularly  check the fluid level of the external waste col -  lection vessel where the sample and solvents are  routed after the vacuum has drained the measur -  ing tubes. Empty the vessel if necessary.  Make sure the tubes are clean and free of particles.  If the Spectro-Visc has been idle for a more than  two days, it is advisable to clean a tube prior to  the first measurement.   Press the “Drain” but -  ton to perform a cleaning cycle. If contaminants  are still present after a cleaning cycle, repeat it by  pushing the “Drain” button again.  Two different functions can be performed with  the Spectro-Visc:  • Viscosity determination (standard)  • Tube calibration  The settings can be configured independently for  each tube. Therefore the user can perform a cali -  bration in one tube while making a viscosity mea -  surement in another.   However, this is not rec -  ommended. During calibration, no other tubes  should be in use to guarantee maximum accuracy.  Although the system has been designed to reduce  temperature variations to the absolute minimum,  the best way to prevent disturbance of a calibra -  tion is to only use the tube to be calibrated.  4.2   CALIBRATION  For calibration a suitable reference oil must be  used. Preferably the viscosity of this reference oil  will be close to the samples you expect to analyze.  Spectro-Visc Automatic Viscometer   |   23  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
The calibration software is also described in Sec -  tion 3.4.1 of this manual, but is repeated here for  convenience.  To enter the “Calibration” mode:  1. From the main menu, Figure 4-1, press the  Function Key “F1”. The screen shown in  Figure 4-2 will appear.  2. Enter the number of the viscosity tube that  you plan to calibrate. The tubes are sequen -  tially numbered from 1 to 4, with the ut -  most left being number “1” and the utmost  right number “4”.  3. Confirm the entry by pressing “ ↵ ”, the  system will then ask for the viscosity of the  reference oil you wish to use during the  calibration.  4. Enter the value of the reference oil using the  numerical entry buttons and the decimal  point entry button.  5. Confirm the entry by pressing “ ↵ ”. The  system will now ask how many determina -  tions will be performed for a calibration.  6. Enter the number of measurements you wish  to perform.  7. Confirm the entry by pressing “ ↵ ”. The  tube number you have entered will now be  in calibration mode and the LCD status  screen will show "Ready for Calibration" for  that respective tube. The process is identical  for all the other tubes.  NOTE!!!  A tube that has been set to “Calibration” mode  will be reset to “Viscosity” mode on the LCD  screen when a calibration cycle has been com -  pleted.  8. Confirm that the “OK” LED for the tube  you are calibrating is ON and that it has  been cleaned.  9. Inject a suitable quantity of the reference oil,  approximately 0.3-0.8 ml (see next para -  graph), into the viscometer tube to be cali -  brated. To inject the sample, use a pipette,  syringe or another kind of dispenser, Figure  4-3. Make sure to inject the sample in a  continuous motion, to prevent air bubbles  getting trapped inside the fluid. The design  of the measuring tubes is such that in most  cases, any air bubbles will escape from the  oil column.  NOTE!!!  For viscosities above 200 cSt (mm 2 /s), use a posi -  tive displacement pipette to ensure the correct  volume is injected each time. OF MEAS:   ! / ESC  Figure 4-3, Inject the Sample into a Viscometer  Tube  24   |   Operation & User Maintenance Manual  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
NOTE!!!  Viscometer tubes must be calibrated at each oper -  ating temperature. The viscometer tube’s con -  stant is dependant on temperature and may vary  significantly if used at a different temperature,  i.e. 40°c and 100°C.  To determine the correct volume of sample do the  following : make sure that the level of the inject -  ed oil does not exceed the horizontal arm of the  viscometer, Figure 4-4. Also, you will need to en -  sure that enough sample is injected so that, when  the oil column has reached the second optical fi -  ber (end of measurement), a quantity of oil still  remains in the horizontal part of the viscometer.  Large capillaries (high constants) require more  sample than small capillaries.  10. Write down the volume used for calibra -  tion and make sure the same quantity is used  with subsequent viscosity determinations.  11. Once the column of oil reaches the first  detection point, the status LED “BUSY”  will light. The LED display will correspond -  ingly show “CALIBRATING Y”, whereas  “Y” is the sequence number of the current  measurement, i.e. if you have set the total  number of measurements to 3 and this is the  second measurement, Y=2. A running timer  will be shown on the screen.  12. When the oil column passes the second  detection point the system determines that  the calibration is either valid or invalid.  Continue with step 13 if it is valid, or step  14 if it is invalid.  13. A calibration measurement is valid when  no air bubbles were detected and the mea -  surement time was within the minimum and  maximum allowed times. The system will  store the result and automatically start to  clean the tube. After the cleaning cycle, the  LCD screen will again show "Ready for cali -  bration". Inject another sample to perform  the next measurement. If necessary, repeat  until the specified number of measurements  has been reached. Upon completion of the  final measurement the LCD Screen will  show the results per the example in Figure  4-5.
