OF MEAS : X Cn=AA.AA OK=' ! ' Co=BB.BB NOT='ESC'  Figure 4-5, Example of Calibration Results  Spectro-Visc Automatic Viscometer   |   25  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
B   = value of constant that is currently stored in  the system’s memory.  Press Enter (“ ↵ ”) to write the newly calculated  constant to the system’s memory or press “Esc” to  keep the currently stored value.  NOTE!!!  If a tube constant has been accidentally overwrit -  ten it can be manually corrected through the  Control Panel, Section 3.4.3.1.  When a printer is attached, it will print out the  individual results of all measurements, as well as  the average and new calculated tube constant.  14. A calibration measurement is invalid when  the “DRAIN” LED for that tube is lit. The  invalid measurement can be the result of  either the measuring time being shorter than  the minimum allowed measurement time, or  because it exceeded the maximum allowed  measurement time. Finally, it can also be  caused by the detection of an air bubble.  The display will show the reason for the er -  ror. Correct the error and repeat the calibra -  tion process with step 1.  Once a full calibration measurement cycle has  been successfully completed, the tube will auto -  matically be reset to standard "viscosity measure -  ment" mode.  NOTE!!!  For optimal results, also make note of which  position (1-4) was used to calibrate the tube.  Aim to use the tube in that position only. If you  place the tube in another unit, re-calibration is  advised.  4.3   VISCOSITY DETERMINATION  This is the standard operating mode for all tubes.  If the Spectro-Visc is switched ON, all tubes will  automatically be in Viscosity mode. The activated  status LED of the respective tube should be “OK”  indicating that the tube has been cleaned and is  awaiting the injection of a sample.  See Section 3.4.2 for information to set the num -  ber of measurements to be performed before the  system prints out the results. The Spectro-Visc is  capable of performing up to 4 simultaneous mea -  surements.  The step-by-step procedure to analyze a used oil  or new oil sample is as follows:  1. The sample is injected into the top of the  viscometer tube, Figure 4-3. The injected  volume should be equal to the volume used  during calibration of that specific tube (see  previous Section 4.2). If the tubes were  purchased with a certificate, a filling volume  is mentioned on the certificate.  After injection, the remaining steps are auto -  matic.  2. The sample flows into the horizontal arm of  the viscometer where it quickly warms up to  the operating temperature. Check the col -  umn of fluid while it is travelling down the  tube. If the oil column still contains visible  bubbles after the beginning of the capillary,  terminate the measurement by pressing the  “Drain” button for the respective tube and  perform a cleaning procedure.  3. The oil then flows into the capillary of the  viscometer on its way to the first optical sen -  sor. When the oil reaches the sensor, the sta -  tus LED of the respective tube will change  to “Busy” indicating that the actual time  measurement has begun. The LED screen,  on the line corresponding to the respective  tube, will show:  “MEAS (Y) T=X”  “Y” is the sequence number of the current mea -  surement, and X is the elapsed measurement time.  If you have set the total number of measurements  to be performed to 3, and this is the second mea -  surement, and after 35 seconds of analysis, the  line would show:  “MEAS (2) T=035.000”  26   |   Operation & User Maintenance Manual  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
4. The sample continues to flow down filling  the measurement chamber entirely.  5. When the sample reaches the second opti -  cal sensor, the timer is stopped. As with the  calibration mode, two scenarios are possible,  the analysis is either valid or invalid. If it is  valid, the sequence continues with step 6. If  it is invalid the system will reject that data,  but will continue and eventually display the  average results of the valid measurements. If  the sample was set to be analyzed only one  time, correct the error and repeat the analy -  sis.  6. A calibration measurement is valid when no  air bubbles were detected and the measure -  ment time was within the minimum and  maximum allowed times. The system will  store the result and automatically start to  clean the tube.  The cleaning cycle starts automatically only if a  printer or computer is attached.   Otherwise the  "DRAIN" button must be pressed after the results  are transcribed from the Status Screen. Solvent is  injected into the tube to clean it and get it ready  for the next sample. The Status Screen shows de -  tailed information indicating the number of in -  jections and the remaining time until the next in -  jection. During cleaning, the Drain LED blinks  constantly.   After the last injection, the vacuum  continues to pull air through the tube to dry it  quickly thereby completing the cleaning cycle.  After the cleaning cycle, the LCD screen will  again show "Ready for measurement". Inject an -  other sample to perform the next measurement.  If necessary, repeat until the specified number of  measurements have been reached. After the final  measurement and if a printer is connected, it will  print out all individual results, or will send them  to the external computer, depending on your  setup. If no printer or computer is attached, the  results will be displayed on the LCD screen.  4.4   CLEANING AND DRYING TUBES  As described in Section 3.4.3.2, the cleaning cycle  consists of the following phases :  • Initial draining period to drain the sample  from the tube.  • A specified number of injections of solvent,  each taking up a specified time, with a speci -  fied interval between subsequent injections.  • Drying time, during which no solvent is  injected and only air is pulled through the  tubes.  After each measurement, the tube must be cleaned  and dried before it can be used for another deter -  mination.  To stop a cleaning cycle in progress, push the  "Drain"   button   of   the   respective   viscometer.  Push the "Drain" button again to complete the  interrupted cycle. If the optional PC software or  printer is connected, the system will automati -  cally start cleaning when a measurement ends.  Note that the system will not start a cleaning cycle  while there are still active measurements taking  place in the tubes. This is to ensure temperature  stability during measurement. This safety feature  can be overridden to clean the respective tube by  simultaneously pushing the “Drain” and “C” but -  tons on the control panel.  NOTE!!!  The system will automatically insert a pause after  6 consecutive cleaning cycles to allow the inter -  nal collection vessel to be emptied. During this  period, no cleaning cycle can be initiated.   After  the 6thc leaning cycle is complete, cleaning will  again be allowed.  Different   samples,   together   with   different  viscometer tubes will require different cleaning  settings.   For example, a high constant tube will  require more solvent while a low constant tube  requires less.   Below you will find some typical  values for the various parameters of the cleaning  cycle :  Spectro-Visc Automatic Viscometer   |   27  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
Draining time : 5 - 60 seconds  The initial draining time will be longer for small  size capillaries because the vacuum needs more  time to drain the sample.   For large capillaries,  this time will be less.  Injection time : 0.3 -1.0 seconds  The time for each solvent injection also depends  on the size of the tube and smaller capillaries will  require less solvent, while larger tubes will need  more. Do not set this value to "0", or the system  will immediately skip to the drying phase of the  cleaning cycle and not inject any solvent.  Number of injections : 5 -10  This largely depends on the type of sample and  the dissolving capacity of the solvent used.  Interval : 5 -15 seconds  For smaller capillaries, the time required to drain  the solvent from the tube will be longer, hence a  longer interval will be required. For large capillar -  ies, this time is significantly shorter.  Drying time : 60-120 seconds  This depends on test temperature and evapora -  tion rate of the solvent used.   Low temperature,  combined with a slowly evaporating solvent will  require more time, while a high test temperature  will facilitate faster drying.   Furthermore, with  smaller capillaries a longer drying time may be  necessary because of the limited air flow through  the tube.  28   |   Operation & User Maintenance Manual  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
Section 5  User  Maintenance  5.1   INTRODUCTION  The following are user maintenance procedures  that should be observed to insure that the Spec -  tro-Visc functions without problems for many  years.  This section details some simple maintenance  procedures that the user can perform.   Please  consult with your supplier or Spectro Inc. if any  other component, not described in this manual,  malfunctions or fails.   Do not try to perform re -  placements or repairs without prior consultation.  First of all, as with all precision instruments, com -  mon sense is the first aspect of good maintenance.  Never use excessive force, damaging tools etc.  when working on the system.  WARNING!!!  Before doing any of the following operations,  make sure that the system is switched off and that  the main power cable of both the system itself and  the thermostat have been disconnected ! Do not  perform any maintenance beyond the procedures  described in this manual unless you have been  qualified and trained by Spectro Inc.  The warranty period of the Spectro-Visc is twelve  (12) months from date of installation or fifteen  (15) months from date of shipment, whichever  occurs first.   Spectro warrants the Spectro-Visc  under conditions of operation against defects of  materials and workmanship.   All defective mate -  rial will   be replaced providing damage was not  caused by improper use.   Warranty applies to  parts and labor only.  5.2   PERIODIC CHECKS  In the Spectro-Visc, connections that in are con -  tact with sample and solvents are of the highest  quality and many design precautions have been  taken to make these as robust as possible. Never -  theless, it remains advisable to periodically check  whether all these connections are still intact and  securely fastened to prevent leaking.  Spectro-Visc Automatic Viscometer   |   29  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
A brief visual inspection can quickly ascertain  that all connections are still in good shape.   In -  spect especially the tubing connectors on the mix -  ing chambers and the fluid drain, Figures 1-1 and  2-3.   Next, using a 2.5 mm Allen wrench, open  the left side of the cabinet, by removing the blue  left side plate which is fastened with 6 hex screws.  Inspect all tubing connections going from the flu -  id drain to the collection vessel, Figure 5-1, from  that vessel to the back of the instrument and all  other tubing for possible leaks.  5.3   REPLACING VISCOMETER TUBES  Replacing the viscometer tubes is a simple and  straightforward task. The step-by-step procedure  to replace any of the tubes is as follows:  1. Pull the solvent dispenser, which is located  directly above the tubes, straight toward you,  removing it from its cradle and place it on  top of the circulator, Figure 5-2.  2. Remove the four aluminum mixing cham -  ber under the tubes by unscrewing the  two screws positioned diagonally across  the bottom of the each chamber using a 4  mm Allen wrench, Figure 5-3. The mixing  chambers can be removed from the bottom  of the bath, but they are still attached to the  drain tubing, so be careful not to damage  the tubing, Figure 5-4.  CAUTION!!!  Note that the mixing chambers can be hot when  the thermostatic bath is operating at high tem -  peratures. If necessary, wear protective gloves.  3. Loosen the bath lid set screw located on the  top front of the bath using the provided 1.5  mm Allen wrench, Figure 5-5.  Figure 5-1, Left Side with Panel Removed  Inspect all Connections  Collection Vessel  Figure 5-3, Remove Screws Holding Mixing  Chamber  Figure 5-2, Solvent Dispenser Raised & Out of  the Way  30   |   Operation & User Maintenance Manual  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
The viscometer tubes need to be raised approxi -  mately 0.5 cm (3/16”) so that the bath cover and  white teflon clamps can be removed. The Spectro-  Visc is delivered with teflon stoppers, Figure 5-6,  which are used to push up the tubes so that the  lid and clamps can be removed (step 4) and when  a tube is removed, they becomes a stopper to plug  the hole at the bottom of the bath in which the  tube resided (step 7).  4. Use one hand to guide the top of a tube, and  with the other hand, press a teflon stopper  against the bottom of each viscometer tube  and carefully push it upward to raise each  tube approximately 0.5 cm (3/16”), Figure  5-7.  WARNING!!  Be careful not to push a tube too far and past the  seal or oil will pour out of the bath. WARNING!!!  Although it is entirely possible to replace the tubes  at higher operating temperatures, there is always  a possibility that oil will leak from the bath at  the moment that the tube is pushed away from its  fitting by the teflon stopper. The quantity of oil  that leaks depends on the speed with which the  movement is carried out. If the operating tem -  perature of the oil is high this could cause skin  burns. Protective gloves should be worn during  this procedure.  8. Extract the viscometer tube from the bath by  pulling it gently up in a straight line, Figure  5-10.  NOTE!!!  Tubes may become stuck and difficult to remove  if they have been in the bath for a long period of  time. If this is the case, try to rotate the tube a  little by grabbing the upper part of the tube near  the funnel and rotate it gently while pushing the  tube and stopper up with the other hand.  5. Remove the four white teflon tube clips and  spacers located between the viscometer tube  neck and the bath cover. To do this, lift a  clip so the flange is out of its hole and push  it in the opposite direction from the opening  in its ring, Figure 5-5.  6. Lift and remove the bath’s lid which consists  of two pieces. First remove the front lid,  Figure 5-8, and then the back portion.  7. Teflon stoppers are used to remove a  viscometer tube and to simultaneously seal  the hole from which the tube was removed.  To remove a viscometer tube, use one of the  teflon stoppers, press against the bottom of  the viscometer and this time push it upward  through the bottom of the bath until the  stopper comes to a stop against the bottom  of the bath chamber, Figure 5-9.  Figure 5-10, Raise and Remove Tube  Figure 5-8, Remove Lids from Top of Bath  Figure 5-9, Push up Stopper to Seal Bath  Use a teflon stopper  and push it up until  it stops against the  bottom of the bath  Guide viscometer tube  with one hand  32   |   Operation & User Maintenance Manual  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
To insert a new viscometer tube, perform the  above procedure in reverse order.   When doing  this, try to gently pull out the stopper and fol -  low slowly with the viscometer, instead of force -  fully pushing down on the viscometer tube.   If  you push the viscometer tube too hard there is  a chance that it will break.   Try to put most of  the force applied on the stopper and not on the  viscometer tube.  Replace the bath lid and tighten the set screw.  Reposition the teflon spacers between the lid and  the viscometers and make sure all four tubes are  pushed down to their normal position. Remount  the mixing chambers. Lower the solvent dispens -  er so that it is again positioned over the tubes.  It may be necessary to re-calibrate the optical sen -  sor after a tube replacement, see Section 5.5.  CAUTION!!!  If a tube breaks when you push it down too hard,  make sure you immediately re-insert the teflon  stopper. If the break occurs beneath the level  of oil and the stopper is not re-inserted, oil will  flow out of the hole until the oil level is below the  break point.  5.4   REPLACING THE OIL  The thermostatic oil will degrade over time due to  temperature effects, turning it darker and dimin -  ishing its heat transfer capacity.   It is difficult to  project when the oil needs to be replaced and this  will largely depend on the test temperature and  usage frequency. Based on daily operation, the oil  should be replaced every 3-6 months.  To replace the oil, perform the same procedure as  when replacing a tube, Section 5.3, steps 1 to 5,  but do not seal the hole with the stopper, instead,  allow the oil to pour out into a suitable vessel.  If necessary, clean the inside of the bath.   To do  this, remove the mixing chambers that are con -  nected to the bottom plate of the bath, and un -  screw the central nut, Figure 5-11. Now, the bot -  tom plate and glass cylinder can be removed and  cleaned.  Make sure when remounting the bath, that the  upper and lower plate are aligned correctly. Nor -  mally, when the same type of oil is used this  cleaning procedure will not be necessary.   How -  ever, when the bath is filled with another type of  oil different from the one previously used, make  sure that all the remaining oil is removed from the  bath before pouring in the new oil.  5.5   CONFIGURING THE OPTICAL  FIBER AMPLIFIERS  5.5.1   Introduction  The optical detection system of the Spectro-Visc  consists of two components, the optical fibers and  the amplifiers. The fibers are the black strands  that run from the sensor housings inside the ther -  mostatic bath to the inside of component cabinet.  Two optical fibers are connected to each sensor  housing detector position. One fiber brings light  to the sensor and the other brings light from the  sensor housing to the amplifier. The amplifiers, to  which the fibers are connected, are located behind  a panel on the right hand side of the Spectro-Visc.  To gain access to the amplifiers, unscrew by hand,  the two screws that hold the panel in place. Open  the hinged panel to view the amplifiers, Figure  5-12.  Figure 5-11, Bottom of Thermostatic Bath  Remove Mixing Chambers  and Central Nut  Spectro-Visc Automatic Viscometer   |   33  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
The optical fiber amplifier will have to be adjusted  in the following situations:  • when placing the system in use for the first  time.  • for differences in light throughput due to dis -  coloration of the thermostatic oil in the bath.  • if the oil during a test is not detected by the  first sensor and the “Busy” LED does not go  ON.  5.5.2   Description  The switching set-point of these amplifiers deter -  mines at which time the time measurements are  started. Figure 5-13 shows a detailed view of the  upper part of the fiber optic amplifiers. At the top  the fiber clamp with which the optical fibers are  secured may be seen. The key components of the  amplifiers are described below:  Detection indicator (orange)   - This indicator is  on when an oil sample is detected.  Light stability indicator (green)   - This indicates  the stability of the light that is received by the  amplifier. It should be a steady green. When the  light output is less than optimal, the LED will  shine less brightly, or even turn off.  Sensitivity scale and adjustment   - The sensitivity  of the amplifier can be changed by turning the  sensitivity adjustment screw, using a small electri -  cal screwdriver.   Turning clockwise increases the  sensitivity, while turning counter-clockwise de -  creases sensitivity.  Detection Indicator (Orange)  Light Stability Indicator (Green)  Sensitivity Scale  Sensitivity Adjustment  Figure 5-13, Optical Amplifiers  Amplifier Number 8  Amplifier Number 1  Figure 5-12, Location of Optical Fiber Amplifiers  34   |   Operation & User Maintenance Manual  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
Timer, mode, operation   - These settings are not  relevant for the operation of the Spectro-Visc and  should be left at the default setting: Timer=off,  Mode=STD, Operation=D  Each Spectro-Visc tube has two detection points,  one for starting the measurement and one for  stopping it.   Each detection point has two fibers  connected to it, one fiber through which the am -  plifier sends light, and one which detects light and  sends it back to the amplifier.   The amplifier de -  tects the oil sample by detecting the change in the  light it receives from the receiving fiber.   When  the sample passes the first detection point, the  corresponding amplifier detects the sample and  starts the time measurement.   Similarly, when  the sample passes the second detection point, the  corresponding amplifier detects it and stops the  measurement.  For each detection point, one amplifier is need -  ed. The amplifiers correspond with the detection  points as shown in Table 5-1.  5.5.3   Adjustment Procedure  Before the amplifiers can be calibrated, the bath  must be filled with bath oil. To allow any air bub -  bles in the bath oil to escape, turn on the thermo -  stat until no bubbles are visible. This needs to be  done only after the bath is first filled with oil.  Adjusting the amplifiers straightforward:  NOTE!!!  Steps 1 to 3 have already been performed during  factory testing and are normally not required.  Only perform these steps if you are unable to get  a detection using steps 4-6.  1. Using a small electrical screwdriver adjust  the sensitivity until the sensitivity scale is  exactly halfway at 6.  2. Release the fiber clamp at the top by push -  ing it backward. This releases the optical  fibers. Now, adjust the position of the front  fiber by slightly moving it up and down. Do  this until both the Detection Indicator LED  (orange) and the Light Stability Indicator  (green) are turned off. Note that usually this  can be achieved by only adjusting the front  fiber, but when you are unable to get both  LED's turned off, also adjust the back fiber.  3. Secure the fiber clamp again and pull the  fibers slightly up to make sure that they are  fitted securely into the clamp. Note that it is  possible that the Detection Indicator and/or  the Light Stability Indicator will turn on  when you secure the clamp, but this is not a  problem for the calibration.  4. Turn the sensitivity adjustment screw coun -  ter-clockwise (decrease sensitivity) until both  the Detection Indicator (orange) and the  Light Stability Indicator (green) turn on.  Then increase sensitivity again by turning  clockwise. First, the Light Stability Indica -  tor will turn off, and then also the Detection  Indicator will turn off.  5. Continue turning clockwise slowly until the  Light Stability Indicator (green) turns on  again. After this happens, continue turning  the adjustment screw for an additional half a  turn (180º) clockwise. The amplifier is now  ready for use.  Amplifier   Tube  Nr. Section 6  Troubleshooting  6.1   SYSTEM TROUBLESHOOTING  In this section, a number of possible problems  will be described, as well as their possible solu -  tions. If the suggestions are not sufficient to solve  your problems, kindly contact your supplier or  Spectro Inc.  1. Symptom: Every measurement is “Invalid”,  display shows “FLOWTIME ERROR”  This is the result of an erroneously bad setting of  the minimal flow time (see Section 3.4.3.3). For  example, if you have set this to 200 sec., every  measurement that is shorter will be regarded by  the system as invalid.  Remedy:   Change the minimal flow time if neces -  sary.  ------  2. Symptom: After cleaning, the “Busy” LED of  a tube lights immediately. This can or cannot be  followed by an “Invalid” result.  This can be the result of a tube that was insuf -  ficiently dried.   Moisture is detected by the op -  tical fiber which will trigger a measurement.   If  the lower detection point is also activated by the  moisture, this will result in an “Invalid” error, ei -  ther because the system will think it has detected  an air bubble, or because the minimal flow time  wasn’t reached.  Remedy:   It can be solved by increasing the drying  time described in Section 3.4.3.2, part E.  Another cause can be that the switching point of  the optical amplifiers has shifted, e.g. by reduced  light throughput as a result of the discoloration  of the thermostatic oil. If you are certain that the  tube is sufficiently dry, readjust the corresponding  amplifier (Section 5.5.3).  ------  3. Symptom: The tube overflows during clean -  ing or the tube drains very slowly when adding  cleaning solvent.  Spectro-Visc Automatic Viscometer   |   37  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
This can be caused by adding too much solvent  during the cleaning cycle.  Remedy:   Reduce the injection time (see Section  3.4.3.2 part B).  Another cause can be that the tube has been  clogged by dirt or particles which were present in  the sample.  Remedy:   Should this be the case, remove the tube  and clean it, by blowing air through it from the  bottom to dislodge any stuck particle.  Remedy:   Alternatively, the initial draining time  may be too short and can be increased to allow  the pump to drain most of the sample before the  first injection of solvent.  ------  4. Symptom: The oil goes past the sensor housing,  but no time measurement is started  This can be the case when the thermostatic bath  oil has degraded and turned dark.  Remedy:   A readjustment of the optical fibers will  be necessary.   Likewise, if a measurement starts,  but is not ended while the oil has clearly passed  the second fiber, readjustment is required.   It is  sufficient to readjust only the fiber that is not de -  tecting the oil.  ------  5. Symptom: The temperature stability is not  within the given specifications  The thermostatic oil may be degraded or an incor -  rect type.  Remedy : Make sure to use a suitable thermostatic  oil with good heat transfer capacity. The viscosity  of the oil is also of the utmost importance : do not  use any oils that exceed a viscosity of 20 cSt in the  operating temperature range.  ------  6. Symptom: The power is on but the instrument  does not seem to be working at all.  The fuse may be blown.  Remedy : Check the fuse that is in the holder be -  low the power cord connection and replace it if  necessary.   Note that one replacement fuse has  been placed in the holder for convenience.  38   |   Operation & User Maintenance Manual  26 August 2008 / Spectro-Visc v.1.4
